verses.flow: There was a man in the land of Uz, whose name was Job; and that man was blameless and upright, one who feared God and turned away from evil.
verses.note: HEBREW: The name 'Iyov (Job, H347) may derive from a root meaning "one who is persecuted" or alternatively from a root meaning "to be hostile," suggesting a passive sense of "the persecuted one." The land of Uz is likely associated with Edom or the Transjordan region (cf. Lam 4:21; Gen 10:23), placing Job outside Israel yet within the sphere of ancient Near Eastern wisdom tradition. The four virtues — blameless (tam), upright (yashar), fearing God (yere elohim), and turning from evil — form a comprehensive moral portrait echoing the Psalms' description of the ideal covenant person (Ps 15; 37:27).

verses.flow: His possessions were seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred female donkeys, and a very great household; so that this man was the greatest of all the people of the east.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The enumeration of livestock — sheep, camels, oxen, and donkeys — reflects the wealth metrics of a semi-nomadic patriarchal chieftain of the ancient Near East, comparable to the patriarchs Abraham (Gen 12:16) and Jacob (Gen 32:5). The designation "greatest of all the people of the east (bene-qedem)" situates Job among legendary eastern sages known throughout the ANE wisdom tradition. Archaeological evidence from the Bronze Age Transjordan region confirms the cultural plausibility of such patriarchal-era wealth structures.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, when the days of feasting had run their course, that Job would send and consecrate them, and he would rise early in the morning and offer burnt offerings according to the number of them all; for Job said, "Perhaps my sons have sinned and cursed God in their hearts." Thus Job did continually all these days.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's role as priestly intercessor for his children foreshadows the covenantal mediatorial function that reaches its fulfillment in Christ's high-priestly intercession (Heb 7:25). The Hebrew verb used for "cursed" (barak, H1288) is used euphemistically here — the same word ordinarily means "blessed," a literary convention employed by scribes to avoid writing "curse God." This ironic double meaning resonates throughout the book, as the adversary later demands that Job do precisely this (v. 11).

verses.flow: Now there came a day when the sons of God (bene ha-elohim) came to present themselves before YHWH, and the adversary (ha-satan) also came among them.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "sons of God" (bene ha-elohim, H1121 + H430) designates members of the heavenly divine assembly, supernatural beings who serve before YHWH — a concept attested throughout the ancient Near East in royal court imagery (1 Kgs 22:19; Ps 82:1; Ps 89:7; Dan 7:10). The term "ha-satan" here carries the definite article, indicating a title — "the adversary" or "the accuser" — rather than a personal name, functioning as a prosecutorial member of the divine council. This scene establishes the cosmic judicial framework within which the entire book of Job operates, and has significant bearing on NT spiritual warfare theology (Rev 12:10).

verses.flow: YHWH said to the adversary (ha-satan), "From where have you come?" The adversary (ha-satan) answered YHWH and said, "From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's interrogation of ha-satan — "From where have you come?" — is not a request for information but a rhetorical device establishing accountability within the divine council. The adversary's answer, "going to and fro (shut) on the earth and walking up and down in it," echoes the commission of patrol beings in Zechariah 1:10–11 and anticipates the NT characterization of the devil as one who "walks about like a roaring lion" (1 Pet 5:8). The scene underscores YHWH's sovereign governance: the adversary reports to YHWH and operates only within permitted boundaries.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to the adversary (ha-satan), "Have you considered my servant Job, that there is none like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man, who fears God and turns away from evil?"
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "my servant (eved) Job" is a title of high distinction in the Hebrew Bible, reserved for figures like Moses (Num 12:7), David (2 Sam 7:5), and the Suffering Servant of Isaiah (Isa 52:13). YHWH himself initiates the subject of Job — it is not the adversary who brings Job to divine attention — which is theologically significant: YHWH commends Job to the heavenly court, making the subsequent trial a divine vindication test rather than merely a satanic accusation. The fourfold character description of v. 1 is repeated verbatim here, underscoring its formal, covenantal weight.

verses.flow: While this one was yet speaking, there came another and said, "The fire of God fell from the heavens and burned up the sheep and the servants, and consumed them; and I alone have escaped to tell you."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The fire of God (esh elohim)" likely refers to a lightning strike or some form of catastrophic electrical storm — a natural phenomenon understood in the ANE as a direct divine act. The Akkadian and Ugaritic literatures frequently portray the storm deity as wielding fire from heaven against enemies, a cultural background that would have given this phrase immediate cosmic weight to its ancient audience. The fourfold structure of the disaster reports (vv. 14–19), each interrupted by a surviving messenger, creates a literary crescendo of mounting catastrophe.

verses.flow: Then Job arose and tore his robe and shaved his head, and he fell on the ground and bowed in worship.
verses.note: HEBREW: The acts of mourning — tearing the robe (qara me'il) and shaving the head (gaz rosho) — are formal gestures of grief well-attested in the Hebrew Bible and ANE culture (Gen 37:34; Isa 22:12; Ezra 9:3). That Job "fell to the ground and bowed (wayyishtachu)" uses the standard Hebrew verb for worship (shachah, H7812), indicating that Job's immediate response to catastrophic loss is prostrate adoration, not rebellion. This response directly confounds the adversary's prediction and establishes Job's integrity before the divine council.

verses.flow: And he said, "Naked I came forth from my mother's womb, and naked I shall return there. YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken away; blessed be the name of YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's doxology — "YHWH gave and YHWH has taken away; blessed be the name of YHWH" — is one of the most profound confessions of divine sovereignty in all of Scripture, affirming that YHWH is the ultimate source and rightful owner of all blessing. The phrase "naked I came forth from my mother's womb and naked I shall return there" employs the womb as a metaphor for the earth itself (cf. Eccl 5:15; 1 Tim 6:7), asserting the utter contingency of human life before the Creator. TYPOLOGY: This voluntary acceptance of loss and suffering as from YHWH's hand prefigures Christ's submission in Gethsemane: "not my will, but yours be done" (Luke 22:42).

verses.flow: In all this Job did not sin, nor did he charge God with wrongdoing (tiphla).
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "tiphla" (H8604), translated "wrongdoing" or "unseemliness," occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible (here; Job 24:12; Jer 23:13) and carries a sense of moral tastelessness, impropriety, or absurdity — a strong word for attributing to God anything unjust or unworthy of His character. The verse functions as a narrative verdict from the divine perspective, vindicating Job before the reader even as his earthly suffering continues. STRUCTURE: This closing verdict forms a literary inclusio with Job 42:7–8, where YHWH again formally vindicates Job's integrity against the misrepresentations of his friends.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to the Adversary, "Have you set your heart upon my servant Job, that there is none like him on the earth — a man blameless and upright, fearing God and turning aside from evil? And he still holds fast to his integrity (tummah), though you incited me against him to swallow him up without cause."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Integrity" (tummah, H8538) derives from the root tamam, meaning wholeness or completeness of moral character. YHWH himself bears witness that Job's suffering was "without cause" (chinnam, H2600) — a theologically explosive admission that righteous people can suffer apart from personal sin. (Rom 8:28, Heb 11:35-38)

Job's physical condition would have marked him as ritually and socially outcast.

verses.flow: Now when Job's three friends heard of all this evil that had come upon him, they came each one from his own place — Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite — and they agreed together to come to condole with him and to comfort him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The three friends represent distinct geographical and likely ethnic traditions: Eliphaz from Teman (a region of Edom renowned for wisdom, cf. Jer 49:7), Bildad from Shuah (associated with Abrahamic lineage, cf. Gen 25:2), and Zophar from Naamah (location debated, possibly northwest Arabia). Their presence reflects an international wisdom tradition surrounding the Job narrative.

verses.flow: And they sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spoke a word to him, for they saw that his suffering (ke'ev) was very great.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The seven-day silence of Job's friends mirrors the seven-day mourning period (shiva) practiced in ancient Israel (Gen 50:10, 1 Sam 31:13). Their initial silent solidarity is the most pastorally sound thing they do in the entire book. The "suffering" (ke'ev, H3511) is rendered here as great, anticipating the anguished speeches to follow. Christ himself is the ultimate righteous sufferer whose friends fled rather than sat. (Isa 53:3, Matt 26:56)

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Leviathan" (Livyatan, Strong's H3882) is the great chaos-dragon of ANE cosmology — cognate with the Ugaritic Lotan — who represents primordial anti-creation forces. "Those skilled to rouse Leviathan" are mythological figures or sorcerers believed capable of unleashing chaos. YHWH's sovereignty over Leviathan is displayed definitively in Job 41, forming a literary inclusion. The divine council theology underlying Job frames these cosmic forces as real but ultimately subordinate to YHWH.

verses.flow: For now I would lie down and be quiet; I would sleep; then I would be at rest —
with kings and counselors of the earth, who built for themselves desolate places,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Desolate places" (chorbot, Strong's H2723) may refer to grand burial monuments, royal tombs, or the ruins of palaces. ANE kings were entombed in monumental structures — pyramids, rock-cut tombs, ziggurat-adjacent burial chambers. Job envisions Sheol as a great equalizer where even the mighty rest in their ruins. Some scholars read this as a reference to kings who "built ruins for themselves," i.e., whose grand works became desolation.

Or why was I not as a hidden stillborn child, as infants who never saw light?
There the wicked cease from turmoil, and there those weary in strength find rest.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's vision of Sheol in vv. 13-19 is notable for its portrayal of the realm of the dead as a place of rest and equality, not punishment. This reflects the common OT understanding of Sheol as the undifferentiated abode of the dead (cf. Eccl 9:10; Ps 89:48). TYPOLOGY: The NT revelation of resurrection and differentiated afterlife (John 5:28-29; Rev 20:11-15) does not contradict but fulfills and clarifies what Job longed for — true rest — which is found ultimately in Christ (Matt 11:28-29; Heb 4:9-11).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Why is light given?" functions as a formal theodicy challenge — Job questions the justice of God's governance in granting life and consciousness to those overwhelmed by suffering. This is not atheism but agonized covenant wrestling, parallel to Jeremiah's confessions (Jer 20:18) and anticipating the Psalms of lament (Ps 88).

verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "fear" is yir'ah (H3374), denoting reverential awe and covenantal fear of God. "Integrity" translates tom (H8537), from tamam, meaning completeness or moral wholeness. Eliphaz ironically appeals to Job's own theology to press his retributive argument, implying Job's suffering indicates a flaw in that integrity.

verses.flow: Remember now: who that was innocent ever perished? And where were the upright cut off?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz here articulates the classic doctrine of retributive justice — the innocent do not perish. While this principle has a covenantal basis (Deut 28), the book of Job is structured precisely to interrogate its mechanical application. The prologue has already revealed that Job's suffering is not retributive, making Eliphaz's confident assertion theologically premature and ultimately rebuked by YHWH in 42:7.

verses.flow: As I have seen, those who plow iniquity and sow trouble reap the same.
By the breath (neshamah) of God they perish, and by the blast of his anger they are consumed.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Breath" here is neshamah (H5397), the same word used in Gen 2:7 for the breath of life YHWH breathed into Adam. Its use here for divine destructive power creates a powerful irony: the same divine breath that animates life can annihilate it. "Blast" translates ruach (H7307), wind or spirit, here denoting wrathful force.

verses.flow: The roaring of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion, the teeth of the young lions are broken.

verses.flow: Now a word was brought to me stealthily; my ear received a whisper of it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Eliphaz's appeal to a nocturnal vision places him within the ANE tradition of oneiric (dream) revelation, attested widely in Mesopotamian literature including the Gilgamesh Epic and Akkadian omen texts. In the ancient Near East, night visions were considered authoritative vehicles of divine communication, lending rhetorical weight to Eliphaz's claim.

verses.flow: dread came upon me, and trembling, and made the multitude of my bones to shake with fear.
A spirit (ruach) passed before my face; the hair of my flesh bristled.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "spirit" (ruach, H7307) that passes before Eliphaz is deliberately ambiguous — it could be a divine messenger, an angel of the heavenly council, or a numinous presence of YHWH himself. The term ruach is used elsewhere for supernatural beings in the divine council (1 Kgs 22:21-23). The terrifying physical reaction (bristling hair, trembling) is consistent with biblical theophanies and angelophanies (Dan 10:7-9; Rev 1:17).

verses.flow: It stood still, but I could not discern its appearance; a form was before my eyes; there was silence, then I heard a voice:
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The speechless, formless vision followed by a voice parallels key moments of divine disclosure in the Hebrew Bible (1 Kgs 19:12; Isa 6). The silence before the voice heightens the transcendence of the revelation. That the form is indiscernible echoes the hiddenness of the divine — no man can see God fully (Exod 33:20). Ironically, YHWH will himself speak from the whirlwind in ch. 38-41, surpassing Eliphaz's secondhand vision.

verses.flow: "Can mortal man be righteous before God? Can a man be pure before his Maker?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Can mortal man be righteous before God?" (ha-yitsdak enosh me-Eloah) is the theological hinge of the entire book. The Hebrew enosh (H582) emphasizes human frailty and mortality. While Eliphaz uses this to diminish Job, the book's resolution vindicates Job's relative righteousness (42:7-8). The question anticipates NT answers: only through the righteousness of the Messiah can any mortal stand before God (Rom 3:21-22).

verses.flow: Even in his servants he puts no trust, and his angels (mal'akim) he charges with error;
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The term mal'akim (H4397, angels / messengers) here refers explicitly to members of YHWH's heavenly assembly. That God charges even his angels with ta'halah (error or folly) demonstrates the absolute transcendence and holiness of the Creator over all created beings, including the divine council members. This aligns with Ps 82, where YHWH judges the elohim of his council, and with the depiction of the satan in Job 1-2 as a council member capable of moral challenge.

verses.flow: how much more those who dwell in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, who are crushed before the moth!
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Houses of clay... foundation in the dust" echoes Gen 2:7 (man formed from the dust, adamah) and anticipates the eschatological resurrection hope. Paul draws on this imagery in 2 Cor 4:7 ("jars of clay") and 5:1 ("earthly tent"). The crushing "before the moth" emphasizes the radical contingency of human existence apart from divine sustaining — a theme Jesus addresses in Matt 6:19-20.

verses.flow: Between morning and evening they are beaten to pieces; without anyone regarding it, they perish forever.
Is not their tent-cord plucked up within them? They die, and that without wisdom."
verses.note: HEBREW: The "tent-cord" (yether, H3499) is a vivid image of the fragility of human life as a tent that collapses when its peg is removed. "Without wisdom" (lo be-chokmah) closes Eliphaz's speech on a note of contempt — humanity dies in ignorance. The irony is profound: YHWH will ultimately rebuke Eliphaz precisely for speaking "without wisdom" (42:7), inverting the charge back upon the accuser.

verses.flow: Call out now — is there anyone who will answer you? And to which of the holy ones will you turn?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The holy ones" (qedoshim, Strong's H6918) refers to the divine council — the heavenly assembly of spiritual beings surrounding YHWH's throne. Eliphaz challenges Job to appeal to any member of this celestial court, implying Job lacks the standing to do so. This reflects widespread ANE and Israelite cosmology in which lesser divine beings served as intermediaries (Ps 89:5-7; Deut 33:2-3; 1 Kgs 22:19). The rhetorical thrust is contemptuous — no intercessor will hear Job.

verses.flow: His harvest the hungry eat up, and he takes it even from among the thorns, and the thirsty pant after his wealth.
For affliction does not come forth from the dust, nor does trouble sprout from the ground,
but man is born to trouble as the sons of the flame (reshef) fly upward.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sons of the flame" (bene reshef, Strong's H7565) is a theologically charged phrase. Reshef was a Canaanite deity of plague and pestilence; in biblical usage the term becomes a common noun for "flame" or "fiery bolt." The phrase may evoke demonic or cosmic forces of destruction flying upward like sparks. Some scholars read this as a proverb about natural calamity; others see an allusion to spiritual agents of affliction. Either reading fits the broader divine council framework of Job (cf. Job 1:6-12). The ESV renders "sparks fly upward," but the literal "sons of reshef" preserves the mythological texture.

verses.flow: But as for me, I would seek God (El), and to God (Elohim) I would commit my cause —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz's counsel to "seek God (El)" is ironically correct advice given from flawed theological premises. Eliphaz assumes Job is suffering because of sin, but the reader knows from chapters 1-2 that Job is suffering precisely because of his righteousness. The name El (Strong's H410), the generic Semitic term for deity, is paired with Elohim (H430), both used here to emphasize divine transcendence and majesty. Eliphaz's theology is not entirely wrong, but it is misapplied — a key dramatic irony of the book.

verses.flow: who does great things and unsearchable, marvelous things without number;

verses.note: HEBREW: Tushiyyah (Strong's H8454) is a rare wisdom term meaning "sound wisdom," "abiding success," or "effective counsel." It appears in Job, Proverbs, Micah, and Isaiah. It carries the sense of competent, enduring achievement rooted in deep understanding. Eliphaz invokes it to describe what the crafty ultimately fail to attain when God frustrates their schemes (cf. Prov 3:21; Job 12:16).

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes this verse directly in 1 Corinthians 3:19 ("He catches the wise in their craftiness"), applying it to the folly of worldly wisdom before the wisdom of God revealed in Christ. This is one of the rare direct NT quotations from Job, confirming its canonical authority. The ultimate fulfillment is the cross itself — the wisdom of God confounding the wise of this age (1 Cor 1:20-25).

verses.flow: Behold, blessed (ashre) is the man whom God (El) reproves; therefore do not despise the discipline (musar) of the Almighty (Shaddai).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz invokes Shaddai (Strong's H7706), "the Almighty," a divine title especially prominent in Job (appearing 31 times versus 17 times in the rest of the OT). The term's etymology is debated — possibly from shadad ("to overpower") or shad ("breast/nourisher"), or from an Akkadian cognate meaning "mountain." Eliphaz's beatitude (ashre, H835 — "blessed is...") echoes the wisdom tradition (Ps 1:1; Prov 3:11-12) and is theologically valid in principle, though misapplied to Job's situation. The same idea is taken up in Hebrews 12:5-11.

verses.flow: For he wounds, but he binds up; he strikes, but his hands heal.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The wounding and binding of verse 18 prefigures the redemptive paradox of Christ's atoning work — the one who was wounded for our transgressions (Isa 53:5) is also the healer of those wounds (1 Pet 2:24). The theme of divine wounding that leads to restoration recurs in Hosea 6:1-2, Deuteronomy 32:39, and throughout the Psalter. In the context of Job, it anticipates the restoration of Job in chapter 42.

verses.flow: In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.
From the scourge of the tongue you will be hidden, and you will not fear destruction when it comes.
At destruction and famine you will laugh, and you will not fear the beasts of the earth.
For your covenant will be with the stones of the field, and the wild animals will be at peace (shalom) with you.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: A "covenant with the stones of the field" (berit even hassadeh) is a striking ANE idiom. In ancient Near Eastern thought, rocks and fields were inhabited by minor spirits or deities; a covenant with the land implies harmony with the created order and its spiritual dimensions. Some interpreters link this to the concept of the land itself as a covenantal partner (Lev 26; Hos 2:18). It anticipates the eschatological shalom in which creation itself is reconciled (Isa 11:6-9; Rom 8:19-22).

verses.note: HEBREW: The image of coming to the grave "in ripe age" (kelach, Strong's H3624 — a word found only here in the OT) conveys the idea of full ripeness or vigor. The hapax legomenon kelach may derive from a root meaning "full strength" or "mature vigor," and the harvest metaphor — dying as a fully ripe sheaf brought in at the proper season — reflects the wisdom tradition's ideal of a complete and blessed life (Prov 9:11; cf. Gen 25:8, Abraham dying "in a good old age").

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The title "Shaddai" (El Shaddai, H7706) appears more frequently in Job than in any other biblical book, occurring some 31 times. The term likely derives from a root meaning "mountain" or "almighty," and conveys the overwhelming power of God. Job's imagery of divine arrows and poison vividly portrays his suffering as a direct assault from God Himself, a theme that resonates with Ps 38:2 and Lam 3:12-13.

verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase rendered "leap for joy" (H5539, salad) is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible — making its precise nuance uncertain. The context suggests an ecstatic trembling or exultation even within pain. Job's clinging to the "words of the Holy One" (imre qadosh) shows that his faith is not in his circumstances but in revealed divine speech.

verses.flow: What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should be patient?

Is it not that my help is nothing within me, and that deliverance has been driven away from me?
"To the one who withholds lovingkindness (hesed) from his friend, he forsakes the fear of the Almighty (Shaddai).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The term hesed (H2617) is one of the richest words in Biblical Hebrew, encompassing loyal covenant love, steadfast kindness, and merciful faithfulness. Job charges that the withholding of hesed from a friend is tantamount to abandoning the fear of God — suggesting that horizontal covenant loyalty reflects vertical covenant fidelity. This anticipates NT teaching on love of neighbor as love of God (Matt 22:37-40).

verses.flow: My brothers have dealt treacherously like a wadi, like the channel of wadis that pass away —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The wadi (nahal) metaphor is deeply rooted in the ANE landscape of Canaan and the Negev, where seasonal streambeds flow vigorously in winter rains but dry up completely in summer heat. Travelers and caravans depended on these water sources; a dried wadi could mean death in the desert. Job uses this striking geographical image to indict his friends as unreliable, seasonal comforters.

verses.flow: Which are dark with ice, and in which the snow hides itself.

verses.flow: The caravans of Tema looked, the traveling companies of Sheba hoped for them.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tema (H8485) is an oasis city in northwestern Arabia, associated with the Ishmaelites (Gen 25:15), and Sheba (H7614) likely refers to the South Arabian trading kingdom famous for its caravans (1 Kgs 10:1-2). These mercantile peoples were renowned for their knowledge of desert travel routes, making the metaphor of their deadly disappointment at a dried wadi especially vivid for an ancient audience.

verses.flow: How forceful are upright words! But what does your reproof reprove?
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "forceful" (H4834, marats) means to press, pain, or be sharp/severe. Job acknowledges that honest, upright words carry real moral weight — but he disputes that Eliphaz's words actually qualify. The rhetorical question cuts to the heart of the dialogue: rebuke must be grounded in evidence, not assumption.

verses.flow: Do you think to reprove words, when the speech of one in despair is as wind?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's description of his speech as the outpouring of one in despair (nophel, "one who falls") reflects the honest lament tradition found throughout the Psalter (e.g., Ps 22, 88). The book of Job validates raw, honest speech to God and about God as a legitimate form of faith, in contrast to the pious platitudes of the friends. James 5:11 cites Job's endurance precisely in the context of honest suffering-speech.

verses.flow: Is there injustice on my tongue? Cannot my palate discern calamity?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job closes his speech with an appeal to moral epistemology — his palate (chek, the sense of taste or discernment) as a metaphor for his capacity to distinguish truth from falsehood. This inverts the friends' assumption that suffering indicates guilt; Job claims his inner moral sense remains uncorrupted and capable of right judgment even in extremity.

verses.flow: Has not a man hard service (tsaba') on earth, and are not his days like the days of a hired laborer?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hard service" translates tsaba' (Strong's H6635), a military term meaning conscripted service or warfare. The word is used in Num 4:23 for temple service and in Isa 40:2 for Jerusalem's "warfare" being ended. Its use here casts human life as a grueling tour of duty, setting the tone for Job's lament as one who has not gone AWOL but is exhausted in legitimate service.

verses.flow: Like a slave who pants for the shadow, and like a hired laborer who waits for his wages,
So I am made to possess months of emptiness, and nights of misery are appointed to me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Months of emptiness" (yarchai-shaw') uses shaw' (H7723), the same word used in the third commandment for taking YHWH's name in vain — denoting that which is hollow, without substance, or deceptive. Job's months are not merely empty but carry the weight of cruel futility. "Nights of misery" (leilot amal) pairs the darkness of night with the heaviest Hebrew word for toil and suffering.

verses.flow: Remember that my life is but a breath (ruach); my eye will not again see good.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Breath" here is ruach (H7307), wind or spirit, evoking the fragility and fleeting nature of life. The appeal "Remember" (zakar, H2142) is a covenantal term — Job is calling on YHWH to act in accordance with covenant memory. TYPOLOGY: This verse anticipates Ps 8:4, "What is man that you are mindful of him?" Job's lament becomes the seedbed for a meditation on divine attention to frail humanity, which finds its ultimate resolution in the Incarnation (Heb 2:6-9).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Sheol (H7585) in Job represents the realm of the dead, the underworld — a place of no return in the experience of the sufferer. Job here voices the conventional ANE understanding of death as a one-way door. This makes the later confession of Job 19:25-27 all the more striking, as hope for resurrection breaks through this despair. HISTORICAL: The imagery of a dissipating cloud was common in ANE lament literature as a metaphor for irreversible death.

verses.note: HEBREW: Two key anthropological terms appear together: ruach (H7307, spirit/breath) and nephesh (H5315, soul/life-force/self). The pairing indicates total personhood — Job's complaint rises from the very core of his being. This is not casual grumbling but the cry of the whole person before God. THEOLOGICAL: Far from being sinful, this raw lament is preserved in canon as a model of honest prayer, consistent with the lament psalms.

verses.flow: Am I the sea, or a sea monster (tannin), that you set a guard over me?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "sea" (yam, H3220) and "sea monster" (tannin, H8577) carry cosmological weight in ancient Semitic thought. In Canaanite mythology, Yam was the sea deity defeated by Baal. In Hebrew theology, YHWH alone commands the chaotic waters (Ps 74:13-14; Job 38:8-11). Job bitterly asks whether YHWH treats him like one of these primordial chaos powers requiring divine containment — a moment of raw theological irony. HISTORICAL: The tannin appears in Egyptian and Ugaritic literature as the chaos dragon; its use here reflects Job's awareness of this mythological framework.

verses.flow: If I have sinned, what have I done to you, O watcher of men? Why have you set me as your target, so that I am a burden to you?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Watcher of men" (notser ha-adam, H5341 + H120) is a striking divine title appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible. It implies constant surveillance. Some manuscripts and versions (LXX) read "watcher of humanity" with a slightly different nuance. Job's question — "why have you set me as your target?" — uses the term miphga' (H4645), a mark or target for arrows, suggesting Job feels singled out for divine assault. THEOLOGICAL: The irony is that from the heavenly council scene (Job 1-2), the reader knows the real adversary is the satan (ha-satan), not YHWH directly — yet Job's perception here is of YHWH as attacker.

verses.flow: Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie down in the dust; and you will seek me diligently, but I shall not be.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's final plea in this chapter — for pardon (nasa', H5375, to lift/carry away) of transgression (pesha', H6588, rebellion) and removal of iniquity (awon, H5771) — uses the full vocabulary of sin and forgiveness found throughout the Psalms and the prophets. TYPOLOGY: The language anticipates the Day of Atonement ritual and ultimately points to Christ, who as the true Kinsman-Redeemer (Job 19:25) would bear the pesha' and awon of His people. The poignant close — "you will seek me diligently, but I shall not be" — is a lament that YHWH's mercy has come too late, a darkness that the resurrection of Christ ultimately answers.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Bildad opens with a rhetorical defense of divine retribution theology, appealing to the justice (mishpat, H4941) and righteousness (tzedaqah, H6666) of El and Shaddai. These two names for God emphasize His sovereign power (Shaddai, "the Almighty" or possibly "Mountain One") and His role as cosmic judge. Bildad's assumption that God's justice is mechanically retributive will ultimately be rebuked by God Himself in chapters 38-42 (Job 42:7).

verses.flow: If your sons sinned against Him, then He sent them away in the hand of their transgression.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Bildad's statement about Job's sons is a blunt and callous application of the retribution principle — that the children's deaths were direct punishment for their sin. This reflects a Second Temple-era wisdom tradition (cf. Sirach 40:10) but goes beyond what the text warrants, and God's verdict in Job 42:7 implicitly condemns such reasoning as falsehood spoken about Him.

verses.flow: If you yourself will seek El (God) and make supplication to Shaddai (the Almighty),

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The appeal to the "former generation" (dor rishon, H1755/H7223) reflects the ANE practice of grounding wisdom in ancestral tradition. Mesopotamian wisdom literature (e.g., the Babylonian Theodicy) similarly appeals to the accumulated teaching of the ancients. The Hebrew word for "searched out" (chaqar, H2713) implies thorough investigation, framing tradition as a kind of empirical inquiry.

verses.flow: for we are but of yesterday and know nothing, for our days upon earth are a shadow —
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "our days upon earth are a shadow" (ki tzel yamenu alei aretz) uses the word tzel (H6738, "shadow") as a metaphor for the brevity and insubstance of human existence. This image recurs throughout the Writings: in Psalm 144:4 man is compared to a breath and a passing shadow. The contrast between the fleeting present generation and the long-accumulated wisdom of the ancients is a rhetorical device to humble Job.

verses.note: HEBREW: The papyrus reed (gome, H1573) and reed-grass (achu, H260) are Egyptian loanwords, suggesting familiarity with Nilotic geography and possibly pointing to the ancient setting of the book. The rhetorical question uses the natural world to illustrate the dependence of the godless upon a substrate — namely, God — which they have abandoned. This nature-wisdom style is characteristic of Solomonic and ANE wisdom traditions.

verses.flow: While it is yet in its blossom and not cut down, it withers before any other plant.
So are the paths of all who forget El (God); and the hope of the godless one shall perish,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The term "godless one" translates chaneph (H2611), meaning one who is profane, polluted, or hypocritically irreligious — stronger than merely "impious." Bildad implies Job may be such a person. The word "hope" (tiqvah, H8615) is significant: in the book of Job, hope becomes a central theological battleground (cf. Job 14:7, 19:10). The loss of tiqvah is the ultimate existential catastrophe for the sufferer.

verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "spider's house" (beit akhavish, H5908) is a vivid image of fragile false security. The word boten (H986, "confidence" or "trust") in the first clause is rare, appearing only here, and may carry the sense of something cut off or severed. The spider's web as a symbol of vain or fragile human endeavor also appears in Isaiah 59:5-6, linking moral corruption to works of structural frailty.

verses.flow: He leans upon his house, but it does not stand; he holds fast to it, but it does not endure.
He is moist before the sun, and his shoots spread over his garden.
His roots are entwined around the stone heap; he looks upon a house of stones.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of roots entwined around stones (gal avanim, a "stone heap") may reflect the agricultural reality of rocky Palestinian soil, where plants force their roots into crevices for moisture. Some scholars see an allusion to the futile persistence of the wicked — their apparent strength is actually a sign of desperate clinging to an unstable foundation. The image anticipates Jesus's parable of the sower and rocky soil (Mark 4:5-6).

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Bildad's declaration that El will not reject the blameless (tam, H8535) is ironically true — but not in the way Bildad intends. The very word tam ("blameless, complete, wholehearted") is used of Job himself in Job 1:1, 8 and 2:3, where God declares Job to be tam. Thus Bildad's principle, correctly applied, should exonerate Job. The ultimate vindication of the blameless man points typologically to Christ, the perfectly blameless Servant (Isa 53:9), whom God did not ultimately abandon.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The promise that God will fill Job's mouth with laughter (sechoq, H7814) and his lips with a shout of joy (terua, H8643) anticipates the restoration described in Job 42:10-17. Terua is elsewhere used of the joyful war-shout or the shout of coronation (Ps 89:15), linking personal restoration to a quasi-royal vindication. This pattern of humiliation followed by exaltation is a recurring typological structure fulfilled in Christ's death and resurrection (Phil 2:8-9).

verses.flow: Then Job answered and said:

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The question "how can a mortal man be in the right before God?" (Hebrew: yitsdaq enosh im-El) is the central jurisprudential and theological problem of the entire book. The verb tsadaq (Strong's H6663) means to be declared righteous, acquitted, or vindicated in a legal sense. Job is not asking whether he can be morally perfect, but whether he can win a lawsuit against God — a theme that recurs throughout the poetic dialogues. This anticipates the NT need for a mediator (1 Tim 2:5).

"Mighty in strength" (amits koach, H533/H3581) describes irresistible sovereign power. The rhetorical question "who has hardened himself against Him and prospered?" echoes the hardening of Pharaoh and implies that resistance to divine sovereignty is futile.

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Who alone stretched out the heavens" (noteh shamayim lebaddo) uses the verb natah (H5186) in participial form, a doxological style common to hymnic passages celebrating YHWH's cosmic sovereignty (cf. Isa 44:24; Ps 104:2). "Trampled the waves of the sea" likely alludes to the ANE combat-myth motif in which the deity subdues the primordial sea (Yam), here repurposed to assert YHWH's uncontested dominion over chaotic powers.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The constellation names — Ash (Bear/Ursa Major), Kesil (Orion, H3685), Kimah (Pleiades, H3598), and "chambers of the south" — reflect ancient Near Eastern astronomical knowledge. These same constellations are referenced in Amos 5:8 and Job 38:31-32, suggesting a shared cosmological vocabulary in Israelite and broader Semitic culture. Their mention underscores God's ordering of the cosmos against the backdrop of chaos.

verses.flow: Who does great things past finding out, and marvelous things without number.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The declaration "who does great things past finding out, and marvelous things without number" (cf. Job 5:9) forms a doxological refrain. The language of niflaot (H6381, "wonders/marvelous things") in the Hebrew Bible consistently points to divine salvific acts — the Exodus, the incarnation, and the resurrection — deeds that exceed human comprehension (Rom 11:33).

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The helpers of Rahab" (ozrey Rahab, H7294) refers to the mythological sea monster and its attendant forces of chaos — a concept directly parallel to the Babylonian Tiamat and her army in the Enuma Elish. In the Hebrew worldview, Rahab represents hostile cosmic powers subordinate to God (cf. Ps 89:10; Isa 51:9). Even these supernatural adversaries must bow beneath YHWH's wrath, reinforcing the divine council framework in which cosmic rebellion is real but ultimately subdued.

It denotes a suppliant's plea for undeserved grace rather than a demand for legal rights. The irony is sharp: Job believes himself righteous (tsaddiq) yet must beg mercy before the very Judge he would face in court — a posture that anticipates the need for an intercessor.

The word chinam (H2600, "without cause, for nothing") appears also in Satan's taunt in Job 1:9 ("Does Job fear God for nothing?") and YHWH's own testimony in 2:3, creating a deliberate verbal link between Job's suffering and its gratuitous character from the divine perspective.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical juxtaposition of "strength" (koach, H3581) and "justice/judgment" (mishpat, H4941) captures Job's dilemma: God is too powerful to be defeated in a contest of might, and too sovereign to be compelled to appear in a court of law. The question "who will summon Him?" (mi yo'ideni) hints at the need for a legal summons — a concept that reaches its resolution only in Christ, the just judge who is also the justifier (Rom 3:26).

verses.flow: Though I am in the right, my own mouth would condemn me; though I am blameless, He would declare me perverse.
I am blameless — I care not for myself; I despise my life.
It is all one — therefore I say, He destroys both the blameless and the wicked.
When disaster brings sudden death, He mocks at the calamity of the innocent.
The earth is given into the hand of the wicked; He covers the faces of its judges — if not He, then who is it?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The earth is given into the hand of the wicked" is one of Job's most daring theodicy statements. He implicitly attributes the moral disorder of the world to divine permission or action. "He covers the faces of its judges" suggests deliberate obstruction of justice. This is not impiety but an anguished cry that anticipates the biblical theme of satanic usurpation of earthly dominion (Luke 4:6; 2 Cor 4:4), which will only be fully reversed at the enthronement of the Messiah.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The shift from third person ("He," vv. 2-24) to second person ("You") is sudden and significant — Job moves from speaking about God to addressing Him directly. This transition from lament to prayer, even in agony, reflects the Psalmic pattern of complaint. It also anticipates the divine confrontation Job will eventually receive in chapters 38-41.

verses.flow: I shall be condemned — why then do I labor in vain?

verses.flow: For He is not a man, as I am, that I might answer Him, that we should come together into judgment.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "He is not a man, as I am" (lo-ish kamoni) is the pivot verse of the chapter. Job's longing is precisely for God to become human so that a fair legal encounter could occur. This is a profound, if unwitting, anticipation of the Incarnation — the Word becoming flesh (John 1:14) so that God and humanity could "come together in judgment" through a righteous mediator (1 Tim 2:5; Heb 4:15).

verses.flow: There is no arbiter (mochiah) between us, who might lay his hand on us both.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The word mochiah (H3198, from yakach, "to argue, decide, mediate, reprove") is rendered "arbiter" or "umpire" — one who lays his hand on both parties to adjudicate their dispute. This is a rare and theologically pregnant term. Job's desperate wish for such a mediator finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, who as both fully God and fully man lays His hand on both parties: "the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself as a ransom for all" (1 Tim 2:5-6; cf. Heb 9:15).

verses.flow: Let Him take His rod away from me, and let not dread of Him terrify me.

verses.flow: Then I would speak and not fear Him, for I am not so in myself."
verses.note: HEBREW: The final phrase "for I am not so in myself" (ki lo-ken anochi immi) is notoriously difficult. It may mean "because I am not thus in my own conscience" (i.e., Job knows himself not to be guilty), or "because it is not so with me" (i.e., the just arbiter is absent and the situation is therefore unjust). Either reading preserves Job's assertion of integrity while acknowledging the existential crisis of a world where the innocent suffer without explanation.

verses.flow: My soul loathes my life; I will give free rein to my complaint against myself; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening phrase "nafshy bechayayi" (my soul loathes my life) uses nephesh (H5315), the full inner self or life-force, expressing Job's existential crisis at its deepest level. The verb qut (H6973), "loathes" or "is weary to disgust," is exceptionally strong — Job is not merely sad but revolted by his own continued existence. This sets the emotional tone for the entire chapter.

verses.flow: I will say to God (Elohim): Do not condemn me; make known to me why You contend with me.

verses.flow: Is it good to You that You oppress, that You despise the work of Your hands, and shine upon the counsel of the wicked?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's question cuts to the heart of theodicy: does God derive benefit (tob, "good") from oppressing His own creation? The phrase "work of Your hands" (maaseh yadekha) frames Job as God's own craftsmanship — making divine affliction seem self-contradictory. Job implicitly accuses God of favoring the wicked's counsel, inverting the moral order Job has always believed in. (Ps 138:8; Isa 64:8)

verses.flow: Do You have eyes of flesh? Or do You see as man sees?
Are Your days as the days of man, or Your years as the days of a mighty man (geber),
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's challenge here is profound — he questions whether God operates under temporal constraints as mortals do, needing to investigate quickly before evidence disappears. The term geber (H1397) denotes a strong, virile man, emphasizing the brevity of human life in contrast to divine eternity. Job is arguing that God's urgency in pursuing him makes no sense given His eternal perspective.

verses.flow: That You seek out my iniquity and search after my sin,
Although You know that I am not guilty, and there is none who can deliver from Your hand?

verses.flow: Your hands fashioned me and made me altogether, yet You destroy me.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "fashioned" (atsab, H6087) implies careful, deliberate shaping, as a craftsman works with his hands. The phrase "altogether round about" (yachad sabib) emphasizes the totality and completeness of God's creative work on Job. The jarring juxtaposition — You made me completely, yet You destroy me — is the theological ache at the heart of Job's lament. (Ps 139:13-16)

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of being made from clay (chomer, H2563) and returning to dust echoes the creation narrative of Genesis 2:7 and anticipates the Resurrection hope — the very clay God shaped He can reshape. This foreshadows the NT affirmation that the same God who creates also redeems and restores. (Gen 2:7; 2 Cor 4:7; Rom 9:21)

Life and loyal-love (hesed) You granted me, and Your visitation guarded my spirit.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word hesed (H2617) is among the richest terms in the Hebrew Bible — covenantal loyal-love, steadfast kindness, faithfulness-in-relationship. Its appearance here is remarkable: Job acknowledges that God granted him hesed even as he laments. "Visitation" (pequddah, H6486) carries the double sense of divine care and divine reckoning, a word that can mean either providential watch-care or accountability. (Lam 3:22-23)

verses.flow: Yet these things You hid in Your heart; I know that this was Your purpose.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "These things You hid in Your heart" suggests Job perceives a hidden divine agenda behind his suffering — a concealed purpose he was not privy to. This verse is theologically significant because it anticipates the divine speeches from the whirlwind (chs. 38-41), where God's hidden wisdom is partially unveiled. It also resonates with the heavenly council scene of ch. 1-2, where God's purposes were indeed hidden from Job. (Job 1:6-12; Rom 11:33)

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job finds himself in a logical trap: guilt means condemnation, but righteousness still brings no relief. This rhetorical bind exposes the inadequacy of strict retributive theology — both outcomes lead to the same misery. The verse anticipates the NT revelation that righteousness before God cannot be self-achieved but must be imputed. (Rom 3:10; Phil 3:9)

The "host" (tsaba, H6635) carries military and heavenly overtones — the same word used for the heavenly armies (tsaba hashamayim). Job may be envisioning himself besieged not merely by circumstance but by organized spiritual forces arrayed against him within the divine council framework. (Job 1:6; 16:19; Ps 82:1)

verses.note: HEBREW: "Shadow of death" translates tsalmaveth (H6757), a compound of tselem (shadow/image) and maveth (death) — a uniquely evocative term describing the deepest darkness, the realm of the dead. It appears also in Psalm 23:4 ("the valley of the shadow of death"), where YHWH's presence transforms even this dread darkness. Job uses it here with no such comfort in view, making the contrast with Ps 23 theologically stark. (Ps 23:4; Matt 4:16)

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's description of Sheol as a land of "no order" (sederim, lit. orders/arrangements) is striking — it is the undoing of creation's ordered light (Gen 1:3). Where God spoke order out of chaos, death returns all to darkness and disorder. The final phrase "where light is as darkness" is a deliberate inversion of creation, signaling total cosmic dissolution from Job's despairing vantage. (Gen 1:2-3; Rev 21:4)

"Should a multitude of words go unanswered, and a man full of talk be justified?
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "justified" is yitsddaq (Strong's H6663, tsadaq), a legal/forensic term meaning to be declared righteous or vindicated. Zophar ironically deploys courtroom language — the same language Job has used to demand a hearing before God — to dismiss Job's claim as mere verbal excess. This sets up the bitter irony of the book: Zophar is technically skilled in theology but utterly wrong in application (Job 42:7).

verses.flow: and that He would tell you the secrets of wisdom! For sound wisdom is double what it appears. Know then that God exacts from you less than your iniquity deserves.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "double what it appears" translates kiplayim (Strong's H3718), meaning "double" or "twofold," modifying the word tushiyyah, rendered "sound wisdom" — an uncommon term implying abiding, efficacious insight or competence (Strong's H8454). Zophar's argument is that hidden divine wisdom, if revealed, would show Job to be even more guilty than apparent. The tragic irony is that God's hidden wisdom, when revealed in the whirlwind, vindicates Job rather than condemning him (Job 38-42).

verses.flow: Can you find out the deep things of God (Elohim)? Can you find out the limit of the Almighty (Shaddai)?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Zophar's rhetorical questions about the unsearchable depths of God (Elohim) contain genuine theological truth — God's ways are indeed inscrutable (Rom 11:33-36; Isa 40:28) — yet they are deployed in a false cause. The names used here are significant: Elohim (the cosmic creator-God) and Shaddai (the Almighty, a name particularly associated with the patriarchal narratives and used dominantly throughout Job). The truth of divine transcendence does not automatically entail Job's guilt.

words.g: from Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: It is higher than the heavens — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The vertical cosmology here — heavens above, Sheol below — reflects the three-tiered Hebrew cosmological worldview (heaven / earth / underworld). Sheol (Strong's H7585) is the realm of the dead, not a place of punishment per se, but of shadow-existence, cut off from YHWH's presence (Ps 6:5; 88:10-12). The rhetorical force is: God's wisdom transcends the entire cosmos; no creature can audit or limit it.

In divine council contexts this term can describe YHWH summoning the heavenly court (Ps 82:1; 89:5-7). The comparison to "a wild donkey's colt born a man" (pere' adam yiwwaled, lit. "a wild ass, a man is born") may be a sarcastic impossibility-saying: a stupid person gaining wisdom is as likely as a wild donkey giving birth to a human being. verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Lifting up the face without defect" anticipates the restoration language that culminates in Job 42. The condition Zophar prescribes — repentance, clean hands, fearless confidence — is ultimately fulfilled not through Job's capitulation to false accusation but through YHWH's own vindication of him. The image of lifting the face without shame resonates with the priestly blessing (Num 6:25-26) and with the face-lifting language of acceptance and covenant favor throughout the Old Testament (Gen 4:7; Ps 4:6).

verses.note: HEBREW: "Misery" here is amal (Strong's H5999), a key word in Job denoting toil, trouble, and the suffering inherent in hard labor and affliction. The image of remembering misery "as waters that have passed away" (mayim avaru) is a beautiful simile of complete dissolution — the suffering will not merely diminish but vanish entirely from memory, as a flood that recedes and leaves no trace. The same word amal recurs throughout Job as shorthand for the totality of his affliction.

This grim final note contrasts sharply with Zophar's offer of restoration to Job in vv. 15-19, underscoring the binary choice Zophar presents: repent and be restored, or perish.

The "spreading abroad" and "abandonment" of nations may allude to the Table of Nations (Gen 10) and the Babel dispersion (Gen 11), when YHWH assigned nations to the sons of God (Deut 32:8, DSS reading). Job's hymn implicitly addresses cosmic governance, not merely earthly politics.

Job's doxology of sovereign divine action anticipates the Psalter's proclamation of YHWH as King over all nations.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: One of the most celebrated confessions of faith in the entire Old Testament. "Though he slay me, yet I will hope in him" — the Hebrew is textually disputed: the Kethib (written text) reads lo (H3808, "not" — "I have no hope") while the Qere (read text) reads lo (H3807a, "in him" — "I will hope"). The Masoretic tradition favors the Qere reading as shown here, yielding a remarkable expression of faith under suffering that anticipates Christ's trust in the Father through death. This is not self-righteousness but covenantal integrity (tamim, wholeness). His access to God's presence itself proves his standing; the impious dare not approach. (Heb 4:16, Ps 15:1-2)

verses.flow: What is man, that he should be clean? And he who is born of a woman, that he should be righteous?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz restates his core theology of universal human sinfulness as the basis for human suffering — no mortal can be clean before God. While theologically grounded (cf. Ps 51:5; Rom 3:23), he misapplies it to deny that innocence can exist in particular cases. The NT affirms the universal principle but also affirms the righteousness of Christ as the exception (2 Cor 5:21).

verses.flow: Behold, God puts no trust in his holy ones (qedoshim), and the heavens are not pure in his sight;
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Holy ones (qedoshim)" — this term refers to the divine council members, the heavenly beings (cf. Ps 89:5-7; Deut 33:2-3; Zech 14:5). Eliphaz invokes the doctrine that even the celestial beings are not fully trusted by God — making the impurity of mortal man all the more emphatic. This is one of the book's key divine council passages, implying a hierarchy of heavenly beings beneath YHWH.

This anticipates the NT ethic of bearing one another's burdens (Gal 6:2) and the critique of hollow religious counsel without genuine compassion.

verses.flow: I was at ease, and He broke me apart; He seized me by the neck and dashed me to pieces; He set me up as His target.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of God setting Job as a "target" (matarah, H4307) reflects ancient military language for archery practice. The same term appears in Lamentations 3:12, suggesting a shared poetic tradition of lamenting divine hostility. This is among the most audacious anthropomorphic accusations in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Gibbor" (H1368) — the term for a mighty warrior or champion, often used of YHWH as the Divine Warrior (Ps 24:8; Isa 42:13). Job's application of this title to God in the context of assault against him is deliberately ironic and theologically charged, inverting the salvation-warfare motif.

verses.flow: I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin and have thrust my horn (qeren) into the dust.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qeren" (H7161) — literally "horn," the symbol of strength, dignity, and honor in Hebrew idiom. To thrust one's horn into the dust is to utterly abase oneself, the reversal of the "exalting of the horn" which signals divine blessing and vindication (1 Sam 2:10; Ps 89:17). Job's act is one of complete humiliation.

verses.flow: O earth, do not cover my blood, and let there be no resting place for my cry!
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cry for the earth not to cover his blood evokes the blood of Abel crying from the ground (Gen 4:10). Job demands that his innocent suffering remain as an open accusation before heaven. The NT applies this typology to Christ, whose shed blood "speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (Heb 12:24), achieving the vindication Job could only cry out for.

verses.flow: Even now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and He who testifies for me is on high.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is one of the most theologically significant in the book. Job, while accusing God, simultaneously appeals to a heavenly witness who will vindicate him — a stunning theological tension. Many interpreters see this as an unconscious prophetic reach toward the mediatorial role of Christ (1 Tim 2:5; Heb 7:25), who intercedes for the accused before the Father. The "witness on high" (ed, H5707) is distinct enough from the God Job accuses to function as a heavenly advocate.

verses.flow: My friends scoff at me; my eye pours out tears to God,

verses.flow: that He would argue the case of a man with God, as a son of man argues with his neighbor.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The desire for one who can "argue the case of a man with God" anticipates the role of the Messianic mediator. The phrase "son of man" (ben-adam, H1121/H120) is the same idiom used in Daniel 7:13 and claimed by Jesus in the Gospels. Job's longing for a divine-human advocate is fulfilled in the incarnate Christ who, being both God and man, can truly mediate between the two (Heb 2:17-18; 1 Tim 2:5).

verses.flow: For when a few years have come, I shall go the way from which I shall not return."
verses.note: HEBREW: "The way from which I shall not return" — derek lo-ashuv (H1870/H7725). This verse closes Job's speech with the finality of Sheol. The word "return" (shuv, H7725) is the same root used for repentance and restoration throughout the Hebrew Bible, making its negation here especially poignant. Job sees no hope of return — setting up the dramatic reversal of the book's conclusion.

verses.flow: My spirit is broken, my days are extinguished, the grave (qever) is ready for me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "My spirit is broken" — ruach (H7307, spirit/breath) is used here with the verb chabel (H2254, to be destroyed/broken). The phrase signals total collapse of vitality. "The grave is ready" (qever, H6913) anticipates burial imminent — Job speaks as one already among the dead. The verse functions as a lament opener establishing existential extremity.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's plea for YHWH to be his surety or pledge (arab, H6148) is remarkable: he asks God to guarantee his case before God. This is the same legal paradox seen in Job 16:19 — the witness against him and the witness for him are both divine. TYPOLOGY: This anticipates Christ as the one Mediator (1 Tim 2:5) who stands as surety for humanity before the Father (Heb 7:22).

verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Byword of the peoples" (mashal, H4912) and spitting in the face were marks of extreme social humiliation in the ANE. To be a mashal was to become a proverbial example of cursed status (cf. Deut 28:37). TYPOLOGY: The language of being spat upon foreshadows the suffering Servant of Isaiah 50:6 and Christ's own passion (Matt 26:67).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 9 stands as a structural pivot — amid Job's lament, a confession of perseverance for the righteous emerges. "Grows stronger and stronger" (yosif omed, lit. "adds strength") suggests progressive fortitude under trial. This anticipates the NT theology of suffering producing endurance (Rom 5:3-4; James 1:3-4).

verses.flow: If I wait, Sheol is my house; I have spread my couch in the darkness.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Sheol (H7585) in Job is portrayed as a realm of inescapable shadow, the underworld domain beneath the divine council's purview. Job imagines spreading his couch there — a posture of resigned habitation. The "bars of Sheol" (v.16) reinforce its character as a locked underworld prison, a concept paralleled in Ugaritic cosmology and later echoed in Revelation 1:18.

verses.flow: I have called to the pit, "You are my father"; to the worm, "My mother" and "My sister."
verses.note: HEBREW: The address to the pit (shachat, H7845, meaning destruction/corruption) as "father" and to the worm (rimmah, H7415) as "mother" and "sister" is a stark rhetorical inversion of family relationship language. Job effectively names decay as his only surviving kin — a poetic declaration that human kinship is dissolving into mortality. The pathos is among the most raw in the entire book.

verses.flow: Shall it go down to the bars of Sheol? Shall we descend together into the dust?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing rhetorical question — "Shall we descend together into the dust?" — leaves Job's lament unresolved. The "bars of Sheol" (baddei sheol) picture Sheol as a locked realm with gates and bolts. TYPOLOGY: Christ's descent into death and His resurrection as the one who holds "the keys of Death and Hades" (Rev 1:18) provides the eschatological answer to Job's despairing question.

(Hebrew: qintzey l'millim) carries the sense of laying snares or traps for words, an ironic foreshadowing of the trap-and-snare imagery that dominates verses 8-10. Bildad accuses Job of manufacturing arguments rather than speaking truth.

verses.flow: You who tear yourself in your anger — shall the earth be forsaken for your sake? Or shall the rock be moved from its place?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Bildad's rhetorical question — "shall the earth be forsaken for your sake?" — reveals the retribution theology underlying all three friends' speeches. They cannot conceive of a cosmos that bends toward the righteous sufferer; for them, the moral order is fixed and impersonal. This stands in stark contrast to the divine response in chapters 38-41.

verses.flow: Indeed, the light of the wicked shall be put out, and the flame of his fire shall not shine.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "light of the wicked" being extinguished is a recurring motif in wisdom literature (Prov 13:9; 24:20). Bildad applies it mechanistically to Job, assuming suffering is proof of wickedness. The irony is that YHWH himself will later vindicate Job's light (Job 42:7-8).

verses.flow: His vigorous steps shall be shortened, and his own counsel shall cast him down.
For he is cast into a net by his own feet, and he walks upon a snare.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word for "net" (resheth, Strong's H7568) and "snare" (s'vakah) belong to the vocabulary of hunting and warfare. The extended trap-and-snare metaphor in verses 8-10 reflects ANE imagery of divine or cosmic judgment ensnaring the guilty, paralleled in Mesopotamian lament literature.

verses.flow: A trap seizes him by the heel; a snare holds him fast.
A rope is hidden for him in the ground, a trap for him upon the path.

verses.flow: Terrors frighten him on every side, and chase him at his heels.
His strength shall be famished, and calamity shall be ready at his side.
It shall devour the members of his skin; the firstborn of death shall devour his members.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The firstborn of death" (bekhor mavet, Strong's H1060 + H4194) is a striking mythological title. In Ugaritic literature, Mot (Death) is a divine figure in the heavenly council with offspring. This phrase may preserve genuine divine council / cosmic enemy language, picturing death as a personified power with a dreadful firstborn agent of consumption. Compare Ps 49:14; Rev 6:8.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: He is torn from his tent in which he trusted, and he is marched to the king of terrors.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The king of terrors" (melekh ballahot) is one of the most evocative titles in the book of Job. It personifies death as a sovereign ruler to whose court the wicked are marched under compulsion. This stands in tension with the NT revelation that Christ has the keys of death and Hades (Rev 1:18), and that the last enemy — death — shall be destroyed (1 Cor 15:26). TYPOLOGY: Job himself, marched unjustly toward death's domain, prefigures Christ who descends into the realm of death only to triumph over it.

verses.flow: In his tent shall dwell that which is none of his; brimstone shall be scattered upon his habitation.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Brimstone scattered upon his habitation" (yizreh al-navahu gofrith) echoes the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24) and became a stock image of divine judgment upon the wicked in ANE and Israelite literature. It signals total and permanent desolation of a dwelling place.

verses.flow: His roots shall be dried up beneath, and above shall his branch be cut off.
His memory shall perish from the earth, and he shall have no name in the street.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The erasure of memory and name (shem) from the earth is the ultimate covenant curse in the ancient Near East — the annihilation of one's legacy and posterity. Compare the promise to the righteous that their name endures forever (Ps 112:6), and the Messianic name that shall be remembered to all generations (Ps 45:17).

verses.flow: He shall be driven from light into darkness, and chased out of the world.
He shall have neither son nor grandson among his people, nor any survivor in his dwelling places.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Neither son nor grandson" translates the Hebrew lo-ven v'lo-neched — literally "no son and no son's son." The term neched (Strong's H5220) is rare, occurring only here and in Gen 21:23. The complete absence of offspring represents the most feared social death in ancient Israelite culture, the antithesis of covenant blessing.

verses.flow: Those of the west shall be appalled at his day, and horror shall seize those of the east.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Those of the west... those of the east" is a merism — a literary device using two polar opposites to express totality. The Hebrew reads literally "those of the latter day... those of the former day" (acharonim... qadmonim), which may also carry a temporal sense: both future and past generations shall be stunned by the fate of the wicked.

verses.flow: Surely such are the dwellings of the unrighteous, and this is the place of him who knows not God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Bildad's closing verdict — "him who knows not God" (lo-yada el) — is deeply ironic in the context of Job. Throughout the prologue, YHWH himself declares Job righteous and calls him "my servant" (1:8; 2:3). Bildad's confident application of this formula to Job constitutes the very theological error for which YHWH later rebukes the three friends (42:7). Knowing God cannot be reduced to conformity with a rigid doctrine of retribution.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's accusation that "Elohim has wronged me" (Hebrew: 'iwwetani, H5791 — bent or perverted) is a stunning theological claim. Job does not deny God's agency in his suffering but protests the apparent injustice. This sets up the legal (rib) framework of the book — Job is bringing a formal complaint before the divine court.

verses.note: HEBREW: "mishpat" (H4941) — justice, judgment, legal right. Job frames his suffering in covenantal legal terms. His cry of "Hamas!" (violence, H2555) echoes the pre-flood moral catastrophe (Gen 6:11), underscoring the extremity of his perceived injustice.

verses.flow: "Oh that my words were written! Oh that they were inscribed in a scroll (sepher)!
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The wish to inscribe words in a "sepher" (scroll, H5612) and in rock with an iron pen reflects genuine ANE archival practices. Royal inscriptions carved into cliff faces (e.g., Behistun inscription) were considered permanent testimony. Job desires his legal complaint to survive as permanent testimony against divine injustice.

words.m: verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: That with an iron pen and lead they were engraved in the rock forever!

words.g: to be the next of kin

verses.flow: For I know that my Redeemer (go'el) lives, and at the last He will stand upon the dust (aphar);
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "go'el" (H1350) — the kinsman-redeemer, the one with legal obligation and right to vindicate, redeem from bondage, or avenge blood. This is the most explicit redeemer declaration in the Wisdom literature. Job's confidence that his go'el "lives" (chai) and "will stand upon the dust" points directly to bodily resurrection and anticipates the incarnation and resurrection of Christ, the ultimate Kinsman-Redeemer (Ruth 4; Rom 8:34; 1 Tim 2:5; Rev 1:18). The NT fulfillment is YHWH's own Son taking flesh to stand upon the earth as the living Vindicator.

verses.flow: And after my skin has been thus destroyed, yet from my flesh I shall see God (Eloah);
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is textually complex (the Hebrew is compressed and some words are debated) but the thrust is bodily resurrection hope: "from my flesh I shall see God." The preposition "min" can mean "from within" or "apart from," generating divergent interpretations, but the context of v.25-27 together strongly supports a resurrection reading endorsed by the early church and reflected in (1 Cor 15:53-54). This is one of the clearest pre-Mosaic testimonies to bodily resurrection in the Hebrew canon.

verses.flow: Whom I myself shall see, and my eyes shall behold, and not another. My heart (kilyot) faints within me!
verses.note: HEBREW: "kilyot" (H3629) — literally "kidneys," the seat of deepest emotion and conscience in Hebrew anthropology, often rendered "heart" or "inward parts." Job's overwhelming emotion at the anticipated vision of God is expressed through visceral, embodied language. His longing is not abstract but physical and personal: "my own eyes" and "not another" stress direct, unmediated beholding.

verses.flow: "If you say, 'How we will pursue him!' and, 'The root of the matter is found in him,'

verses.flow: Be afraid of the sword yourselves, for wrath brings the punishment of the sword, that you may know there is a judgment (din)."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's closing warning invokes "din" (H1779 — judgment, legal verdict), completing the legal/courtroom motif that structures the chapter. Having declared his Redeemer and his coming vindication, Job turns on his accusers with a sober eschatological warning: divine judgment is real and the sword of wrath awaits those who falsely condemn the innocent. Compare (Matt 7:1-2) and (Rom 2:1).

verses.flow: "Therefore my troubled thoughts answer me, and on account of my haste within me.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "my troubled thoughts" renders the Hebrew sikhot (שִׂיכוֹת), from a root suggesting anxious rumination or disputatious inner deliberation. Zophar presents his reply as emotionally compelled, not merely rational. The phrase "haste within me" (khushi bi, חוּשִׁי בִי) conveys a visceral, urgent agitation, suggesting Zophar feels personally insulted by Job's prior speech (ch. 19).

verses.flow: I hear reproof that dishonors me, and a spirit (ruach) beyond my understanding answers me.
Do you not know this from of old, since man was placed upon earth,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Zophar's appeal to antiquity ("from of old... since man was placed upon earth") reflects a common ANE rhetorical strategy of grounding wisdom claims in primordial, universal tradition. This echoes wisdom traditions found in Sumerian and Akkadian texts that likewise anchor moral truths in cosmic origins.

verses.flow: that the rejoicing of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless but for a moment?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "rejoicing of the wicked is short" encapsulates the classical retribution theology that all three friends defend. This is not false in itself — Scripture affirms the transience of wicked prosperity (Ps 37:35-36; Prov 24:20) — but Zophar misapplies it as an absolute and immediate mechanism, presupposing Job is wicked. The book of Job ultimately deconstructs this mechanical application without overturning the broader truth.

verses.flow: Though his pride mount up to the heavens, and his head reach to the clouds,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of pride mounting "to the heavens" with the head "reaching to the clouds" echoes the hubris of Babel (Gen 11:4) and the boast of the king of Babylon in Isaiah 14:13-14. Both passages link overreaching pride to catastrophic downfall, a pattern culminating in the eschatological humiliation of all who exalt themselves against God (Phil 2:8-11).

verses.flow: he will perish forever like his own dung; those who have seen him will say, "Where is he?"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Like his own dung" (kegelalо, כְּגֶלְלוֹ) is a deliberately coarse image; the Hebrew root galal is related to the word for dung or refuse. This is one of the more visceral insults in the poetic sections of Job. The rhetorical question "Where is he?" (אַיּוֹ) parallels the sudden vanishing of the wicked in Ps 37:10.

verses.note: HEBREW: "The venom of asps" (mererath petanim, מְרֵרַת פְּתָנִים) uses the word mererah, meaning "gall" or "bitterness," pointing to the bile-like poison of serpents. The image of sweetness turning to venom in the stomach is a powerful reversal motif: what the wicked relishes externally becomes internally destructive, a vivid picture of sin's deceptive pleasure yielding death (Prov 9:17-18; Rom 6:23).

verses.flow: He swallows down riches and vomits them up; God casts them out of his belly.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "God casts them out of his belly" introduces the divine agency explicitly into Zophar's retribution theology. Even the physical undoing of the wicked is attributed directly to God's action. While theologically correct that God governs outcomes, Zophar's error is certainty of timing and application — he assumes this mechanism is already operating against Job.

verses.flow: For he has crushed and abandoned the poor; he has seized a house that he did not build.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Zophar identifies the specific sins underlying the wicked man's doom: oppression of the poor and unjust seizure of property. These are classic covenant violations condemned throughout the Torah (Deut 24:14-15) and the Prophets (Amos 2:6-7; Mic 2:2). Ironically, these accusations mirror what Job will later be charged with directly by Eliphaz (Job 22:6-9), though Job repeatedly denies them (Job 29:12-16; 31:13-22).

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of God "raining" his burning anger upon a person resonates with the divine warrior imagery common in ANE and biblical texts (cf. Ps 11:6; Gen 19:24). The concept of divine wrath sent down upon the wicked reflects the heavenly council's executive function — the enactment of divine decrees of judgment from the cosmic court upon earthly wrongdoers.

verses.flow: All darkness is stored up for his treasures; a fire not fanned by man will consume him; it will devour what is left in his tent.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "A fire not fanned by man" (esh lo-nupakh, אֵשׁ לֹא-נֻפָּח) distinguishes the consuming judgment as supernaturally originated, beyond human cause. This fire imagery evokes eschatological judgment themes (Isa 66:24; Mark 9:48). The storage of "darkness" for the wicked as treasure is a bitter irony — their accumulated wealth becomes accumulated doom.

verses.flow: The heavens will reveal his iniquity, and the earth will rise up against him.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The heavens will reveal his iniquity, and the earth will rise up against him" presents a cosmological witness motif: both the heavenly realm and the earthly creation testify against the wicked. This reflects the divine council's role as cosmic judiciary, with creation itself serving as witness and prosecutor (cf. Deut 32:1; Mic 6:1-2; Ps 50:4-6).

verses.flow: The produce of his house will be carried away, flowing off in the day of God's wrath.

verses.flow: This is the portion of a wicked man from God, and the heritage appointed for him by God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The portion of a wicked man from God" and "the heritage appointed for him by God" form a powerful closing parallelism. The term "heritage" (nakhalah, נַחֲלָה) normally refers to the covenantal inheritance granted to the righteous (Ps 16:5-6), making its application to the wicked's doom a sharp ironic reversal. Zophar ends with rhetorical confidence, but the reader knows from the prologue that he has fundamentally misread Job's situation.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruach" (Strong's H7307) here carries the sense of spirit or breath — Job's complaint is not ultimately with mortals but with Eloah himself, making his anguish of the deepest existential and theological order.

verses.flow: When I remember, I am troubled, and trembling seizes my flesh.
Why do the wicked live, grow old, yes, become mighty in power?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job here launches his most direct counter-argument to the retribution theology of his three friends (Eliphaz, Bildad, Zophar). Their entire system rests on the premise that the wicked suffer and the righteous prosper; Job demolishes this with raw empirical observation. This tension is not resolved until YHWH speaks from the whirlwind (Job 38).

verses.flow: Their seed is established with them in their sight, and their offspring before their eyes.
Their houses are peace (shalom) from dread, and the rod of Eloah is not upon them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shalom" (Strong's H7965) here denotes not merely peace but wholeness, security, and undisturbed wellbeing. The irony is profound: the wicked experience the shalom that Deuteronomic theology reserves for the covenant-faithful. "Eloah" (H433) is a poetic singular form of Elohim, characteristic of the book of Job.

verses.flow: His bull breeds and does not fail; his cow calves and does not miscarry.

verses.flow: They wear out their days in prosperity, and in a moment they go down to Sheol.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sheol" (H7585) is the Hebrew underworld, the realm of the dead, conceived as a place of shadows and silence. Job observes that the wicked transition to Sheol not in prolonged suffering but "in a moment" (berega') — further undermining simple retributionist frameworks.
