verses.flow: And they say to El, "Depart from us! We do not desire the knowledge of your ways.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The speech of the wicked in verses 14–15 is a direct rejection of covenant relationship. The language "Depart from us" (sur mimmennu) echoes the rebellious posture of those who sever themselves from divine instruction, prefiguring the "fool" of Psalm 14:1 who says in his heart there is no Eloah.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 1st common plur + suffix

verses.flow: What is the Almighty (Shaddai) that we should serve him? And what profit is it that we should entreat him?"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shaddai" (Strong's H7706), traditionally rendered "the Almighty," is one of the most frequent divine titles in Job (appearing ~31 times). Its exact etymology is debated — possible connections to shadad (destruction/power) or shad (breast/nourisher) — but in Job it consistently denotes the sovereign, all-powerful deity.

verses.flow: Behold, is not their prosperity in their hand? The counsel of the wicked is far from me.

verses.flow: They are like straw before the wind, and like chaff that the storm steals away.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The imagery of the wicked as chaff driven by wind appears also in Psalm 1:4, connecting Job's observation with the broader wisdom tradition. The NT picks up this winnowing motif in Matthew 3:12, where the Messiah himself is the one who separates wheat from chaff at final judgment.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: "Eloah stores up his iniquity for his sons." Let him repay it to him so that he may know it.
Let his own eyes see his destruction, and let him drink of the wrath of Shaddai.
For what is his delight in his house after him, when the number of his months is cut off?
Can anyone teach El knowledge, seeing that he judges those who are on high?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "those who are on high" (ramim) may carry a nuanced reference to exalted beings — possibly members of the divine council — whom YHWH judges. This echoes Psalm 82:1 where Elohim judges in the divine assembly, and Isaiah 24:21 where YHWH punishes "the host of heaven on high." Job's rhetorical question concedes that El's judicial perspective transcends all human categories.

verses.flow: One dies in his full strength, being wholly at ease and quiet.

verses.flow: And another dies in bitterness of soul, and has never eaten with good.
Together they lie down in the dust, and the worm covers them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The leveling of the prosperous and the bitter in a common grave (dust and worms) anticipates the eschatological equality of death, which the NT reframes through resurrection hope (1 Cor 15:54–55). For Job, death as equalizer offers cold comfort but no ultimate resolution.

verses.flow: "Behold, I know your thoughts, and the schemes by which you would wrong me.
For you say, 'Where is the house of the noble? And where is the tent, the dwelling place of the wicked?'
Have you not asked those who pass along the roads? And do you not regard their signs?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Those who pass along the roads" refers to traveling merchants and caravaneers who, in the ancient Near East, served as conveyors of international wisdom and proverbial lore. Job appeals to universally observable human experience — not merely Israelite tradition — to validate his argument, reflecting the international character of wisdom literature.

verses.flow: That the evil man is spared in the day of calamity; that he is rescued in the day of wrath.
Who declares his way to his face? And who repays him for what he has done?
Yet he is borne to the grave, and over the tomb a watch is kept.
The clods of the valley are sweet to him; and all men draw after him, and those before him are without number.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 2nd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: How then will you comfort me with empty words? For your answers remain only faithlessness (ma'al)."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ma'al" (Strong's H4604) denotes treachery, faithlessness, or a breach of trust — a strong word implying the friends' answers constitute not merely error but a kind of covenant-betrayal in their misrepresentation of divine justice. Job closes his speech by indicting their theology as fundamentally empty and unfaithful.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said:
Can a man be profitable to God? Surely he who is wise is profitable to himself.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz opens with a rhetorical question challenging Job's presumption of moral leverage over God. The Hebrew verb 'sakan' (H5532) means "to be of use or benefit," implying a commercial or transactional relationship. Eliphaz's retributive theology assumes that human righteousness produces no benefit for God, thus God has no motive to favor Job unless Job has sinned. This framing sets up the entire false accusation that follows. (Rom 11:35)

verses.flow: Is it any pleasure to the Almighty (Shaddai) that you are righteous? Or is it gain to Him that you make your ways blameless?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Almighty" renders 'Shaddai' (H7706), a divine name of disputed etymology — possibly from 'shadad' (overwhelming power) or 'shad' (breast, nourisher), or from an Akkadian cognate meaning "mountain." In Job, Shaddai appears more than in any other biblical book (31 times), suggesting an ancient patriarchal divine name tradition. Eliphaz's use of it here is ironic: he invokes divine transcendence to diminish Job's claims.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Is it for your fear of Him that He reproves you — that He enters into judgment with you?
Is not your evil great? And there is no end to your iniquities.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz transitions from rhetorical questions to direct accusation — a significant move. He asserts Job's guilt as established fact without any evidence. The word 'rabbah' (great/much) modifies evil, and 'ein qetz' (no end) modifies iniquity, using emphatic constructions. This is the most severe condemnation any of the three friends levels against Job, making Eliphaz's eventual rebuke by YHWH in 42:7 theologically pointed.

verses.flow: For you have taken pledges from your brothers for nothing, and stripped the naked of their clothing.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Pledges of clothing as collateral for loans are attested in ANE law codes. The Mosaic law (Exod 22:26-27; Deut 24:12-13) forbade keeping a poor man's outer garment overnight because it served as his only covering. Eliphaz fabricates specific social crimes to explain Job's suffering, projecting the retributive logic backward from suffering to sin. Archaeology has confirmed the socioeconomic practices of debt-pledge in Iron Age Canaan.

verses.flow: You have given no water to the weary to drink, and from the hungry you have withheld bread.
The man of strong arm — the land belonged to him, and the man of lifted countenance dwelt in it.
You have sent away widows empty-handed, and the arms of the fatherless were crushed.

verses.flow: Therefore snares are around you, and sudden dread terrifies you,

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: or darkness so that you cannot see, and a flood of waters covers you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Is not God high in the heavens? And see the summit of the stars, how exalted they are!
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Eliphaz appeals to cosmic geography — God is in the "height of the heavens" (gobah shamayim) and the stars are exalted. In Second Temple and ANE cosmology, the stars were associated with divine beings or the heavenly host (cf. Job 38:7; Isa 14:13; Ps 82). Eliphaz uses this to argue that God is too distant to monitor human affairs — ironically echoing the very skepticism he accuses Job of holding (v.13-14). (Isa 40:22)

verses.flow: And you have said, "What does God know? Can He judge through the deep darkness?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz now puts words in Job's mouth — a rhetorical accusation that Job has secretly reasoned that God cannot see through the darkness to judge accurately. The word 'araphel' (thick darkness, H6205) is the same word used of the theophanic darkness at Sinai (Exod 20:21) and of YHWH's dwelling (1 Kgs 8:12). Ironically, YHWH does speak to Job from the storm (38:1), demonstrating that divine knowledge is not obscured.

verses.flow: Thick clouds veil Him so that He cannot see, and He walks on the vault of heaven."
Will you keep to the ancient path which wicked men have trodden?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "ancient path" ('orach 'olam') echoes wisdom tradition vocabulary; 'olam (H5769) can mean "ancient time" or "eternity." Eliphaz invokes the paradigm of the antediluvian wicked, likely alluding to the generation of the Flood. This is the same retributive pattern used throughout wisdom literature — the fate of the wicked as a warning. (Jer 6:16)

verses.flow: They were snatched away before their time; their foundation was poured out as a river.
They said to God, "Depart from us," and "What can the Almighty (Shaddai) do to them?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The taunt "Depart from us" ('sur mimmennu') mirrors the language of Psalm 1:1 ('resha'im', the wicked) and recalls the defiant self-sufficiency of those who reject divine governance. The rhetorical question "What can Shaddai do to them?" is the voice of practical atheism — not philosophical denial but functional dismissal of divine sovereignty. This anticipates YHWH's own speeches showing cosmic mastery (chapters 38-41).

verses.flow: Acquaint yourself now with Him, and be at peace (shalom); thereby good will come to you.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Acquaint yourself" renders 'saken-na' from 'sakan' (H5532) — the same root used in v.2 regarding being "profitable" to God. Eliphaz uses ironic wordplay: he began by asking whether man can profit God; now he urges Job to "acquaint/profit" himself through submission. "Be at peace" is 'shalom' (H7965) — wholeness, completeness, well-being. The call to shalom here is theologically sound even if Eliphaz's diagnosis is wrong, as YHWH ultimately does restore Job's shalom. (Jas 4:8)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Receive, I pray, instruction from His mouth, and lay up His words in your heart.
If you return to the Almighty (Shaddai), you will be built up; if you put iniquity far from your tent,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The call to "return to the Almighty" ('shuv el-Shaddai') uses the standard Hebrew word for repentance — 'shuv' (H7725), to turn back. While Eliphaz applies it wrongly to Job (who has not sinned as charged), the theological principle points to the covenant pattern: return and restoration. This language anticipates the prophetic call to repentance (Isa 55:7; Joel 2:12-13) and ultimately the gospel summons to return to the Father. (Luke 15:18)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: and lay your gold in the dust, and the gold of Ophir among the stones of the torrent-bed,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ophir (H211) was a legendary source of the finest gold in the ancient world; its exact location is debated — candidates include southern Arabia, East Africa (Somalia/Zimbabwe), and even India. Ophir gold is mentioned in 1 Kings 9:28; 10:11; Ps 45:9; Isa 13:12. The instruction to cast gold into the dust reflects the wisdom tradition's subordination of material wealth to the fear of YHWH (Prov 3:14; Job 28:15-17).

verses.flow: You will make prayer to Him, and He will hear you, and you will pay your vows.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Eliphaz promises that restored relationship with God will result in answered prayer and fulfilled vows — a genuinely biblical covenantal pattern (Ps 50:14-15; 91:15). The irony of the Book of Job is that YHWH ultimately commands Eliphaz himself to ask Job to pray for him (42:8), and Job's prayer restores Eliphaz's standing. The theology of intercession thus comes full circle in the book's resolution. (Jas 5:16)

verses.flow: You will decide on a matter, and it will be established for you, and light will shine on your ways.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: When they cast down, you will say, "There is lifting up," and He saves the one of downcast eyes.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew of this verse is notoriously difficult. "When they cast down" may render 'ki-hashpilu' — a Hiphil form of 'shaphel' (H8213, to be low/humble). "Lifting up" is 'ge'ut' (H1466), exaltation or pride. The phrase "one of downcast eyes" ('shephal 'einayim') denotes the humble. The verse may mean: in the hour of humiliation, God exalts the lowly — a theme central to the Magnificat (Luke 1:52) and the Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-5). (1 Sam 2:7-8)

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He delivers even the one who is not innocent; he is delivered through the cleanness of your hands.
verses.note: HEBREW: The final verse presents significant textual difficulty. The Masoretic Text reads 'lo-naqi' ("not innocent/not clean"), while some manuscripts and the LXX support 'lo-naqi' differently or read 'lo' as "to him." The phrase "cleanness of your hands" ('bar kappayim') uses 'bar' (H1249, pure, clean) — closely related to the "pure in heart" of Ps 24:4. Eliphaz promises intercessory power to the restored Job, an ironic prophecy: Job does indeed intercede for Eliphaz in 42:8-9. (Job 42:8)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Job answered and said:
"Even today my complaint is bitter; my hand is heavy upon my groaning.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "bitter" is מַר (mar, Strong's H4751), carrying the sense of extreme anguish or gall. "My hand is heavy" (יָדִי כָּבְדָה, yadi kavdah) is a striking idiom — some manuscripts read "his hand" (יָדוֹ), referring to God's hand upon Job, which would intensify the theological tension of divine affliction and may be the preferred reading.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Oh that I knew where I might find him, that I might come even to his dwelling place!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's cry "Oh that I knew where I might find him" (מִי-יִתֵּן יָדַעְתִּי, mi-yitten yadati) is not atheism but anguished theism — he desires direct access to God, not escape from him. This yearning for divine encounter prefigures the mediatorial role of Christ, the one Mediator between God and man (1 Tim 2:5), through whom access to the Father is now freely given (Heb 4:16).

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: I would lay out before him my case, and fill my mouth with arguments.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common sing

verses.flow: I would know the words he would answer me, and understand what he would say to me.
Would he contend with me in the greatness of his power? No — surely he would set it upon me.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "greatness of his power" (רָב-כֹּחַ, rav-koach) underscores the vast asymmetry between Job and God. The clause "he would set it upon me" (יָשִׂם בִּי, yasim bi) is interpretively difficult — Job seems to hope that God would give him full attention rather than overwhelm him with omnipotent force, implying a desire for genuine legal engagement rather than sheer divine coercion.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel cohortative 1st common sing
preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: There an upright man could reason with him, and I would be delivered forever from my judge.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "An upright man" (יָשָׁר, yashar) could reason or argue his case before God — Job insists on his moral integrity as the basis for his appeal. The term "judge" (שֹׁפְטִי, shopti) is significant; Job oscillates between seeing God as adversary and as the only possible vindicator. This courtroom imagery permeates the book and reaches its climax in Job's oath of innocence (Job 31) and ultimately in God's verdict from the whirlwind (Job 38–41).

verses.flow: Behold, I go forward, but he is not there; and backward, but I cannot perceive him;

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: on the left hand, where he works, but I cannot behold him; he turns to the right hand, but I cannot see him.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: But he knows the way that is with me; when he has tested me, I shall come forth as gold.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "He has tested me, I shall come forth as gold" (כַּזָּהָב אֵצֵא, kazahav etze) is one of the great refining-fire passages of the Old Testament. The image of divine testing through suffering that produces proven, purified character runs through the Psalter (Ps 66:10), the Prophets (Isa 48:10; Mal 3:3), and into the New Testament (1 Pet 1:6–7; Jas 1:3), where the testing of faith is explicitly compared to the refining of gold.

verses.flow: My foot has held fast to his steps; his way I have kept, and have not turned aside.
From the commandment of his lips I have not departed; in the treasure of my bosom I have laid up the words of his mouth.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "in the treasure of my bosom" (מֵחֻקִּי, mechukki) is a hapax legomenon or rare expression — the Hebrew חֵק (cheq) refers to the bosom or chest, the intimate interior of a person. Job claims he has hidden (lit. "treasured," צָפַנְתִּי, tsafanti) God's words deeper than his prescribed portion (חֹק, choq, meaning "statute" or "allotted portion"). This suggests that divine instruction is not merely external law but an interiorized treasure, anticipating the new covenant promise of the law written on the heart (Jer 31:33).

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: But he is in one mind, and who can turn him back? And what his soul desires, that he does.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "He is in one mind" (בְּאֶחָד, be'echad) — God is absolutely singular and unchangeable in his sovereign will. The rhetorical question "who can turn him back?" (וּמִי יְשִׁיבֶנּוּ, umi yeshivenu) echoes divine sovereignty language found throughout the Psalms and Isaiah (Isa 43:13; 46:10). This is not fatalism but a recognition of God's unchallengeable sovereignty — the very attribute that simultaneously terrifies Job and yet grounds his hope.

verses.flow: For he will complete what is appointed for me, and many such things are with him.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Many such things are with him" (כְּאֵלֶּה רַבּוֹת עִמּוֹ, ke'elleh rabbot immo) may hint at the heavenly retinue and the council's agenda for Job's life — decisions made in the divine assembly (Job 1–2) that are enacted in the earthly realm without Job's knowledge. This underscores the divine council backdrop of the entire book: Job suffers from policies established in the heavenly court, unbeknownst to him.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Therefore I am terrified before him; when I consider, I am in dread of him.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: And God has made my heart faint, and the Almighty has terrified me;
verses.note: HEBREW: "The Almighty" translates שַׁדַּי (Shaddai, Strong's H7706), the archaic divine epithet prominent in patriarchal narratives and throughout Job (appearing over 30 times). Its etymology is disputed — possibly from שָׁדַד (shadad, "to overpower") or a cognate with a Semitic root for "mountain," suggesting the God of the high places or the All-Sufficient One. In Job, Shaddai consistently appears in contexts of raw divine power confronting human frailty.

verses.flow: for I was not cut off before the darkness, nor before thick darkness that covers my face."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing verse is syntactically dense and the negation (לֹא, lo) is textually contested. Job seems to assert that despite his terror, it was not the darkness itself that silenced him — his complaint arises precisely because he refuses to be swallowed by obscurity without a hearing. This defiant persistence in lamentation is itself an act of faith, the very "steadfastness of Job" commended in (Jas 5:11).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix
conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: Why are times not hidden from the Almighty (Shaddai), and they who know him do not see his days?
verses.note: HEBREW: "times" ('ittim, H6256) refers to appointed seasons or set times of divine judgment. "Shaddai" (H7706), often rendered "Almighty," likely derives from shadad ("to overpower") or shad ("mountain/breast"), conveying God's sovereign sufficiency. Job's complaint is that God's appointed days of judgment are not visible to human observers, a tension central to theodicy in wisdom literature.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: The wicked remove boundary stones; they seize flocks and pasture them.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Removal of boundary stones (gevulot, H1367) was a serious crime in the ancient Near East, condemned in Deuteronomy 19:14 and 27:17. Such stones marked property boundaries and protected the inheritance of families. This act was associated with covenant curse in ANE treaty traditions and Mosaic law alike.

verses.flow: They drive away the donkey of the fatherless; they take the widow's ox as a pledge.
They thrust the poor off the road; the afflicted of the earth all hide themselves.
Behold, like wild donkeys in the wilderness they go out to their toil, seeking prey in the desert as food for their young.
verses.note: HEBREW: The comparison of the poor to wild donkeys (pere', H6501) is ambiguous — it may describe the desperate freedom of the destitute who forage like animals, or it may describe the wicked exploiting the poor as beasts of burden. The LXX and many commentators favor the former reading, depicting the suffering poor reduced to animal-like survival.

verses.flow: They reap in a field not their own, and they glean the vineyard of the wicked.
They lie naked through the night without clothing, and have no covering in the cold.

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: They are drenched by the mountain rains and cling to the rock for want of shelter.
There are those who snatch the fatherless child from the breast, and take a pledge against the poor.
They go about naked, without clothing; hungry, they carry the sheaves.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: Between the rows of olives they press out oil; they tread the winepresses, yet suffer thirst.

words.g: from + in/with a city in the widest sense
and a deity or the Deity

verses.flow: From the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries out; yet God charges no one with wrong.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "yet God charges no one with wrong" (lo' yasim tipelah, lit. "does not set folly/blame") is one of the most theologically charged statements in Job. It implies divine inattention to injustice, echoing Job's earlier accusations. The word tipelah (H8604) means unsavoriness, folly, or moral impropriety. This verse sharpens the theodicy crisis at the heart of the book.

verses.flow: There are those who rebel against the light, who do not know its ways and do not stay in its paths.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Those who rebel against the light" introduces a motif of moral darkness as spiritual rebellion. Light is a consistent theological symbol for divine order and revelation (cf. Ps 36:9; John 1:5). The three classes that follow — murderer, adulterer, thief — are defined by their affinity for darkness, inverting the created order of Genesis 1.

verses.flow: The murderer rises before dawn; he kills the poor and needy, and in the night he is like a thief.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: The eye of the adulterer watches for the twilight, saying, "No eye will see me," and he veils his face.
In the dark they dig through houses; by day they shut themselves in — they do not know the light.
For deep darkness is morning to all of them; for they are friends with the terrors of deep darkness.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Deep darkness" translates tsalmaveth (H6757), a compound of tsel ("shadow") and maveth ("death") — literally "shadow of death." This term appears memorably in Psalm 23:4. It is used in Job with striking frequency (3:5; 10:21-22; 16:16; 24:17) to describe the realm of chaos, death, and divine absence.

verses.flow: He is swift upon the face of the waters; their portion is cursed in the land; no treader turns toward their vineyards.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Drought and heat snatch away the snow waters; so does Sheol those who have sinned.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The comparison of Sheol (H7585) to drought consuming snow waters is a powerful wisdom image. Sheol in the Hebrew Bible is the underworld abode of the dead, not yet developed into the full afterlife theology of the intertestamental period. The verse asserts that sinners will be consumed by death as inevitably as heat dissolves snow — though within Job's argument, this functions ironically, since the wicked seem to escape such retribution.

verses.flow: The womb forgets them; the worm finds them sweet; they are remembered no more; and wickedness is broken like a tree.
They wrong the barren woman who does not bear, and do no good to the widow.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Yet God drags away the mighty by his power; he rises up and no one is assured of life.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The mighty" (abbirim, H47) can refer to the powerful elite, but the term is also used of divine beings or heavenly warriors (cf. Ps 78:25 where angels are called "mighty ones"). Some scholars (Heiser, Day) detect a double reference here — God's sovereign power operates over both human rulers and superhuman powers, consistent with the divine council backdrop of Job's prologue (Job 1-2).

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He gives them security, and they are supported; and his eyes are upon their ways.

words.m: conjunction + verb hophal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: They are exalted a little while, and then are gone; they are brought low and gathered up like all others; they are cut off like the heads of grain.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of the wicked being "cut off like the heads of grain" anticipates harvest imagery used for divine judgment throughout prophetic and wisdom literature (cf. Isa 17:5; Matt 13:30). This fleeting exaltation followed by sudden cutting down becomes a pattern applied eschatologically to the final judgment of the enemies of God's Messiah (Ps 37:35-36; Rev 14:14-16).

conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And if it is not so, who will prove me a liar and make my speech worth nothing?
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Job closes chapter 24 with a direct rhetorical challenge to his interlocutors, asserting the veracity of his observations. This challenge mirrors his earlier oath-formulae and anticipates the climactic oath of innocence in chapters 29-31. The formula "who will prove me a liar" (yakziveni, from kazav, H3576) is a courtroom idiom, reinforcing the forensic legal metaphor that runs throughout the book.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
"Dominion and dread are with Him; He makes peace (shalom) in His high places.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Bildad opens with a declaration of divine sovereignty — dominion (mishal) and terror (pachad) characterize YHWH's rule. The word "shalom" (peace/wholeness) in His high places likely refers to the ordered governance of the heavenly realm, implying that cosmic harmony flows downward from YHWH's throne. This sets up his argument that no creature can stand before such a God.

verses.flow: Is there any number to His troops? And upon whom does His light not rise?
How then can a man be righteous before God (El)? And how can one born of woman be pure?
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "born of woman" (yalud ishah) is a recurring idiom in Job (Job 14:1; Job 15:14) emphasizing human frailty and creaturely limitation. The verb "yitzdak" (be righteous, Strong's H6663) is the same root used in legal contexts — Bildad frames human righteousness as a forensic impossibility before an infinite God. Compare Paul's use of similar logic in Romans 3:20.

verses.flow: Behold, even the moon has no brightness, and the stars are not pure in His eyes—
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The declaration that even the moon and stars lack brightness before God reflects the Hebrew cosmological worldview in which heavenly bodies were associated with the divine assembly (cf. Deut 32:8; Ps 82:1; Job 38:7). The stars "are not pure" (lo zakou) echoes the motif that even members of the heavenly host cannot stand in God's absolute holiness — a background assumption of divine council theology.

verses.flow: How much less man, who is a maggot, and the son of man, who is a worm!"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The description of man as a "maggot" (rimmah, Strong's H7415) and a "worm" (tola'at, Strong's H8438) anticipates the language of Psalm 22:6, where the suffering righteous one cries, "I am a worm and not a man." This Psalm is explicitly fulfilled in Christ's crucifixion, suggesting that Bildad's crushing rhetoric ironically points toward the One who would fully bear human lowliness and redeem it (Phil 2:7-8).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Job answered and said:
"How you have helped him who has no power! How you have saved the arm that has no strength!
How you have counseled him who has no wisdom, and plentifully declared understanding!
With whose help have you uttered words, and whose breath (neshamah) came out from you?
verses.note: HEBREW: "neshamah" (H5397) refers to the breath or spirit of life, the same word used in Gen 2:7 when YHWH breathes life into Adam. Bildad's theological discourse is here mocked by Job as empty of divine origin — whose neshamah, Job asks, truly animates such speech?

words.g: and to reside or permanently stay
words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: The Rephaim (Repha'im) tremble beneath the waters, and their inhabitants.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "Repha'im" (H7496) are the shades of the dead, possibly connected to the ancient giant clans of Canaan. Their trembling beneath the cosmic waters evokes a three-tiered cosmology in which the underworld realm lies beneath the primordial sea, and its inhabitants are subject to divine authority.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Sheol (She'ol) is naked before him, and Abaddon (Abaddon) has no covering.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "She'ol" (H7585) and "Abaddon" (H11) appear together as a merism for the realm of death in its totality — She'ol as the abode of the dead, Abaddon meaning "destruction" or "ruin." Both are "naked" (arom) and uncovered before YHWH, affirming that no realm lies outside his omniscience. (Rev 9:11) uses Abaddon as a name for the angel of the abyss.

verses.flow: He stretches out the north (tsaphon) over the void, and hangs the earth upon nothing.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "He hangs the earth upon nothing (tohu)" — the Hebrew "belimah" (H1099) means literally "without anything," a remarkable anticipation of cosmic suspension with no visible support. "Tsaphon" (H6828), the north, carries cosmic-mythological weight in ANE thought as the mountain seat of divine assembly (cf. Ps 48:2; Isa 14:13), suggesting Job may be alluding to divine council geography.

verses.flow: He binds up the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud is not split open under them.
He covers the face of the full moon, and spreads over it his cloud.
He has inscribed a circle upon the face of the waters, at the boundary between light and darkness.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: The pillars of heaven tremble and are astonished at his rebuke.

words.g: the a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: By his power he stilled the sea, and by his understanding he shattered Rahab (Rahab).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Rahab" (H7294) here is not the woman of Jericho but a cosmic sea-dragon figure representing primordial chaos, cognate with Babylonian Tiamat and Ugaritic Yam-Nahar traditions. YHWH's "shattering" of Rahab echoes the creation-by-combat motif of the ancient Near East, reframed in Hebrew theology as absolute divine sovereignty. (Isa 51:9; Ps 89:10)

verses.flow: By his wind (ruach) the heavens were made beautiful; his hand pierced the fleeing serpent.
verses.note: HEBREW: "ruach" (H7307) is wind, breath, or spirit — here most naturally rendered "wind" in the context of cosmic creation, yet the ambiguity is intentional: the same divine ruach that hovers over the waters in Gen 1:2 beautifies the heavens. "The fleeing serpent" (nachash bariach, H5175 + H1280) is a chaos-monster figure cognate with Ugaritic Lotan, the precursor to biblical Leviathan. (Isa 27:1)

verses.flow: Behold, these are but the outskirts of his ways, and how small a whisper do we hear of him! But the thunder of his power — who can understand?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The climactic verse of Job's doxology, deploying "outskirts" (qetsot, H7099 — edges or fringes) to convey that all of creation's wonders — the cosmic ocean, the underworld, the hanging earth, the shattered sea-dragon — are but the hem of YHWH's garment. The "thunder of his power" (ra'am geburotav) is incomprehensible to human minds, a profound statement of divine transcendence that contextualizes the entire dialogue of the book.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And Job continued his discourse (mashal) and said,
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "mashal" (H4912) denotes a formal discourse, proverb, or extended poetic speech — not merely casual words. Its use here signals that Job's following speech carries deliberate, weighty rhetorical force. This is the third such mashal-discourse ascribed to Job (cf. 26:1; 29:1), framing his words as wisdom speech rather than emotional outburst.

verses.flow: "As God (El) lives, who has turned aside my right (mishpat), and the Almighty (Shaddai), who has made my soul bitter,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's oath sworn by the life of El who has wronged him is theologically striking — he calls upon God as witness against what he perceives as God's injustice toward him. Shaddai (H7706, "the Almighty") is the patriarchal name for God prominent throughout Job, evoking the Abrahamic covenantal context (Gen 17:1). Job's bitter soul does not drive him to abandon God but to confront him — a posture of agonized faithfulness.

verses.flow: for as long as my breath (neshamah) is in me, and the spirit (ruach) of God is in my nostrils,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Neshamah" (H5397) is the breath of life breathed into Adam at creation (Gen 2:7), here linked with "ruach" (H7307, spirit/breath) in the nostrils — a double affirmation of divinely-given vitality. Job grounds his moral resolve in the very life-force God placed in him, making integrity inseparable from creatureliness. TYPOLOGY: The dual neshamah/ruach pairing recalls Gen 2:7 and anticipates the Spirit-breathed new creation life of the redeemed (John 20:22; 2 Tim 3:16).

verses.flow: my lips will not speak what is wrong, nor will my tongue utter deceit.
Far be it from me to declare you right — until I expire I will not put away my integrity (tummah) from me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tummah" (H8538) denotes wholeness or moral completeness — the same root as "tam" used of Job in 1:1 ("blameless"). Job's refusal to relinquish his integrity even under pressure is the central dramatic irony of the book, as God himself had affirmed this very quality (1:8; 2:3). His self-defense is not prideful but covenantally grounded.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: My righteousness (tsedaqah) I hold fast and will not let it go; my heart does not reproach me for any of my days.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpolel participle active + suffix

verses.flow: Let my enemy be as the wicked, and let him who rises against me be as the unrighteous.
For what is the hope of the godless when he is cut off, when God takes away his soul (nephesh)?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question concerning the hope of the godless (chaneph, H2611 — one who is profane or polluted) at death is central to the friends' theology that Job is now ironically adopting. The nephesh (H5315, soul/life) being "taken away" by God points to divine sovereignty over death. The godless have no genuine hope because they have no genuine relationship with God to sustain them beyond the grave.

verses.flow: Will God hear his cry when distress comes upon him?
Will he delight himself in the Almighty (Shaddai)? Will he call upon God at all times?
I will instruct you concerning the hand of God; what is with the Almighty (Shaddai) I will not conceal.
Behold, all of you have seen it yourselves — why then have you become utterly vain (hebel)?
This is the portion of the wicked man with God, and the inheritance (nachalah) of the violent (aritsim) that they receive from the Almighty (Shaddai):
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nachalah" (H5159) is the covenantal term for inheritance or allotted portion — used here darkly to describe what the violent receive from Shaddai, i.e., judgment rather than blessing. "Aritsim" (H6184) refers to violent, terrifying men. The irony is that the same covenantal framework that promises blessing to the faithful promises ruin to the oppressor. THEOLOGICAL: Job here appears to appropriate the friends' doctrine of retribution — but whether he speaks it sincerely or sarcastically to expose its inadequacy remains a major interpretive debate among commentators.

verses.flow: If his sons are multiplied, it is for the sword, and his offspring will not have bread enough.
Those who survive him will be buried in death, and his widows will not weep.
Though he heaps up silver like dust, and piles up clothing like clay —
he may pile it up, but the righteous will wear it, and the innocent will divide the silver.

words.g: and + as/like a hut or lair

verses.flow: He builds his house like a moth, like a booth (sukkah) that a watchman makes.
verses.note: HEBREW: The comparison of the wicked man's house to a moth's dwelling and a "sukkah" (H5521, a temporary booth or tent) powerfully contrasts the apparent solidity of wealth with its fragility. The sukkah is the temporary shelter of harvest workers, deliberately impermanent. TYPOLOGY: The image of the booth resonates with Israel's wilderness sojourn and the Feast of Booths (Lev 23:42-43), where impermanence is a theological statement about dependence on God rather than earthly security.

verses.flow: He lies down rich, and he will not do so again; he opens his eyes, and it is no more.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: Terrors overtake him like a flood; in the night a whirlwind carries him off.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Terrors" (ballahoth, H1091) overtaking the wicked like a flood echoes the chaos-waters imagery of the ancient Near East, where overwhelming disaster is expressed through the motif of inundating waters. This same term is used of the terrors that have attacked Job himself (18:11; 24:17), suggesting that the very fate Job's friends attributed to the wicked has apparently befallen the innocent — which is precisely the problem the book of Job confronts.

verses.flow: The east wind lifts him up and he is gone, and it sweeps him out of his place.

verses.flow: It hurls itself at him without pity; from its power he flees headlong.

verses.flow: It claps its hands at him and hisses at him from its place."
verses.note: HEBREW: The subject of the clapping and hissing in verse 23 is grammatically ambiguous in the Hebrew — it may be God, the storm wind, or onlooking men who mock the fallen wicked. The verbs "clap the hands" and "hiss" are gestures of contempt and derision in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Lam 2:15; Ezek 27:36). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Some interpreters read the hissing and clapping as actions of divine or heavenly agents executing judgment upon the wicked — consistent with the broader divine council framework in which celestial beings carry out YHWH's decrees of judgment (cf. Ps 82:8; Deut 32:8).

verses.flow: Surely there is a mine for silver, and a place for gold that they refine.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Job 28 functions as a distinct hymn to wisdom often called the "Wisdom Poem," standing apart from the dialogue cycle as a reflective interlude. Its authorial voice is disputed — whether it represents Job's own meditation or an independent insertion — but it pivots the entire book toward the climactic divine speeches in chapters 38-41, where YHWH himself answers from the whirlwind.

verses.flow: Iron is taken from the dust, and copper is smelted from the ore.
Man puts an end to darkness, and to the farthest limit he searches out the ore in gloom and deep darkness.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mining imagery in verses 1-11 reflects detailed knowledge of ancient Near Eastern mining operations, including the deep-shaft silver mines of the Sinai and Arabah regions. Archaeological evidence from Timna and Wadi Faynan confirms that shaft mining with oil lamps (reflected in "man puts an end to darkness") was practiced from the Bronze Age onward, lending the poem remarkable historical concreteness.

verses.flow: He opens a shaft away from where men sojourn; they are forgotten by the foot — they hang far from men, they swing to and fro.

Its stones are the place of sapphires, and it has dust of gold.

verses.flow: Man puts his hand to the flinty rock; he overturns the mountains from their roots.
He cuts out channels through the rocks, and his eye sees every precious thing.
He dams up the weeping of the streams, and the hidden thing he brings forth to light.
But wisdom (chokmah) — where shall it be found? And where is the place of understanding (binah)?
verses.note: HEBREW: The key term chokmah (Strong's H2451) denotes practical wisdom, skill, and the moral-intellectual capacity to live well before God. Its parallel binah (H998) emphasizes discernment and the capacity to distinguish between things. The rhetorical question "where shall it be found?" (Hebrew: me-ayin) echoes the cosmic search motif and sets up the poem's central theological claim: wisdom is not a commodity discoverable by human industry.

verses.flow: Man does not know its worth, and it is not found in the land of the living.

verses.flow: The deep (tehom) says, "It is not in me," and the sea says, "It is not with me."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Tehom (the deep, Strong's H8415) is the primordial cosmic ocean of Genesis 1:2, personified here as a respondent to the wisdom-search. In the Ugaritic and broader Semitic mythological background, Yam (Sea) and Tehom represent cosmic forces associated with chaos. Their explicit denial of possessing wisdom underscores that wisdom transcends even the deep structural powers of creation — it belongs to YHWH alone.

verses.flow: Gold cannot be given in exchange for it, nor silver weighed out as its price.
It cannot be valued in the gold of Ophir, in precious onyx or sapphire.

verses.flow: Gold and glass cannot equal it, nor can vessels of fine gold be exchanged for it.
No mention shall be made of coral or crystal; indeed, the acquisition of wisdom is above pearls.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The topaz of Cush cannot equal it, and it cannot be valued in pure gold.
Then from where does wisdom come? And where is the place of understanding?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The repetition of the refrain from verse 12 ("from where does wisdom come?") creates a deliberate literary bracket (inclusio) around the survey of creation's inability to yield wisdom. This rhetorical structure drives the reader toward the only possible answer: wisdom has its source exclusively in the Creator, not in creation. This anticipates YHWH's challenge to Job in chapters 38-41: "Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth?"

verses.flow: It is hidden from the eyes of all living, and concealed from the birds of the heavens.
Abaddon and Death say, "With our ears we have heard a rumor of it."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Abaddon (Strong's H11, "destruction" or "place of ruin") appears here alongside Death (Mavet) as personified cosmic powers who have only "heard a rumor" of wisdom. In the Hebrew cosmological schema, these are the powers of the underworld realm (Sheol). Their partial, rumor-based knowledge contrasts sharply with God's direct and complete comprehension of wisdom in verse 23, reinforcing the absolute ontological gap between YHWH and all other powers.

verses.flow: God (Elohim) understands the way to it, and he knows its place.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The shift from the universal negative ("no creature knows") to the singular positive affirmation "Elohim understands the way to it" is the theological apex of the chapter. The Hebrew verb bin (to understand, discern) used of God here echoes the noun binah from verse 12, forming a wordplay: only the God who is the source of understanding truly understands wisdom's location. This is not merely omniscience but ontological identity — wisdom is coextensive with God's own nature.

verses.flow: For he looks to the ends of the earth, and sees under the whole heavens.
When he gave to the wind its weight, and apportioned the waters by measure,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The description of God weighing the wind and apportioning waters by measure (vv. 25-26) directly anticipates YHWH's speeches in Job 38:4-38 and carries strong resonance with Proverbs 8:27-29, where personified Wisdom is present at the ordering of creation. The NT identifies Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24) and the agent of creation (Col 1:16-17), making this passage a significant typological pointer to the pre-incarnate Son through whom all things were ordered.

verses.flow: And he said to man, "Behold, the fear of the Lord (Adonai) — that is wisdom (chokmah), and to turn away from evil is understanding (binah)."
verses.note: HEBREW: The chapter's climax introduces Adonai (Lord, H136) rather than YHWH or Elohim, a solemn and elevated divine title. The phrase "fear of Adonai" (yirat Adonai) is the classical wisdom formula shared with Proverbs 1:7 and 9:10, and Psalm 111:10, forming a canonical wisdom triad. The two-part definition — fear of God as wisdom, departure from evil as understanding — deliberately mirrors the two terms of verse 12's question, giving a precise divine answer to the human search. This verse is not merely pious conclusion but the interpretive key to Job's entire ordeal: his fear of God (1:1, 1:8) is itself the deepest form of wisdom, even when he cannot comprehend his own suffering.

verses.flow: And Job continued his discourse (mashal) and said,
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "mashal" (Strong's H4912) denotes a discourse, parable, or weighty saying, not merely casual speech. Job's use here signals a formal, elevated lament structured as a wisdom poem. The chapter functions as a nostalgic retrospective of his former honor, contrasting sharply with the humiliation of ch. 30.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's longing is not primarily for material prosperity but for the experience of divine watchfulness (shamar, H8104 — to guard, keep). This covenantal language echoes the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24) and reflects the intimate relationship Job once enjoyed with Eloah, a singular form used frequently in Job to stress personal deity.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Choreph" (H2779) is rendered "harvest-prime" or "autumn of my life" by various scholars; it carries connotations of ripeness and fullness of season. "Sod" (H5475) means intimate council or fellowship — the same word used in Ps 89:7 of the divine council assembly, here applied to the intimacy of divine friendship over Job's household.

verses.flow: when Shaddai was yet with me, and my children were around me,
when my steps were bathed in cream, and the rock poured out for me streams of oil,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of steps bathed in cream (chemah, H2529, curdled milk or butter) and rocks streaming with oil reflects the agricultural abundance of the ancient Near East as a sign of covenantal blessing (Deut 32:13; 33:24). Such hyperbolic abundance language parallels Egyptian and Ugaritic royal blessing texts.

To "prepare a seat" at the gate was to hold recognized judicial and social authority (cf. Ruth 4:1-2; Prov 31:23).

verses.flow: for when an ear heard, it called me blessed, and when an eye saw, it bore witness to me,
because I delivered the poor who cried out, and the fatherless who had no helper,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Job's deliverance of the poor, the fatherless, and the widow foreshadows the messianic ministry of Yeshua, who declared his anointing to preach good news to the poor and liberty to the oppressed (Luke 4:18; Isa 61:1). The threefold concern — the crying poor, the fatherless, the widow — is the hallmark of covenantal justice in Torah (Exod 22:22-23; Deut 10:18).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The clothing metaphor for righteousness (tsedaqah, H6666) and justice (mishpat, H4941) is deeply covenantal. Job's self-description as wearing righteousness like a garment anticipates the priestly and royal investiture language applied to the Messiah (Isa 11:5; 59:17; Rev 19:8). The turban (tsaniyph, H6797) is a term used elsewhere for the high priest's headpiece, suggesting a priestly dimension to Job's role.

verses.flow: and I broke the fangs of the unrighteous, and from his teeth I cast out the prey,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The language of breaking fangs and rescuing prey from teeth evokes combat with a devouring adversary — language later applied to spiritual warfare and the defeat of the enemy (Ps 3:7; 58:6). Job portrays his former advocacy as militant rescue, aligned with YHWH's own role as defender of the oppressed.

verses.flow: then I said, 'I shall die in my nest, and I shall multiply my days as the sand,'
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "die in my nest" (qen, H7064) reflects a common ANE metaphor for dying peacefully at home surrounded by one's household. "Multiply days as the sand" is a hyperbolic expression of long life. Some scholars emend "sand" (chol, H2344) to "phoenix" (chol, H2343) based on a homonym, reading this as an allusion to the mythological bird of renewal — a reading with some ancient versional support but uncertain in MT.

verses.flow: my root was spread out to the waters, and the dew lodged overnight upon my branch,
my glory (kavod) was fresh with me, and my bow was renewed in my hand,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Kavod" (H3519) — glory, honor, weighty presence — is the same word used for the divine glory that fills the tabernacle and temple (Exod 40:34). For Job to say his kavod was "fresh" (chadash, H2319, renewed/new) suggests a vitality and dignified honor continuously replenished. The renewed bow (qeshet, H7198) is a standard ANE metaphor for sustained martial and royal vigor.

verses.flow: and they waited for me as for the rain, and they opened their mouth wide as for the spring rain (malqosh),
verses.note: HEBREW: "Malqosh" (H4456) is the spring or latter rain, which in the agricultural calendar of ancient Israel fell in March-April and was essential for the final ripening of grain. Its pairing with the early rain (yoreh) marked the full provision of YHWH across the growing season (Deut 11:14; Joel 2:23). The crowd's eagerness for Job's words as for life-giving rain elevates his counsel to near-prophetic status.

verses.flow: I chose out their way and sat as chief, and I dwelt as a king in the army, as one who comforts mourners."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Job's description of himself as one who "sat as chief" and "comforted mourners" resonates with the royal-priestly ideal ultimately fulfilled in the Messiah, who is both King of kings and the one who comforts all who mourn (Isa 61:2-3; Matt 5:4). The phrase "as a king in the army" (kammelekh bagedud) echoes the warrior-king motif of Davidic messianism, while the comforting of mourners points to the suffering servant's ministry (Isa 53:4).

verses.flow: But now they laugh at me, those who are younger than I in days, whose fathers I disdained to set with the dogs of my flock.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The social reversal in v.1 is stark — Job, who once sat at the city gate as a respected elder (ch. 29), is now mocked by men whose own fathers were too base for his shepherd-dogs. ANE honor-shame culture made such public humiliation tantamount to social death. The phrase "younger than I in days" (tze'irim mimmenni le-yamim) emphasizes the generational inversion of the shame.

verses.flow: What to me was the strength of their hands? Upon them vigor (koach) had perished.

Among the bushes they brayed; under the nettles they huddled together.
Sons of the senseless, indeed sons without a name, they were driven out from the land.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sons of senseless men" (bene-nabal) echoes the root nabal (H5036), the same word used of the fool who "says in his heart there is no God" (Ps 14:1) and the name of Abigail's churlish husband (1 Sam 25). "Sons without a name" (bene-lo-shem) indicates those stripped of all social identity and ancestral honor — the most complete form of disgrace in the ancient Near East.

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The referent of "he" is deliberately ambiguous — it may be YHWH who loosed Job's cord (yeter, the bowstring or tent-cord, H3499), implying divine withdrawal of restraint and protection. This connects to the heavenly council prologue (chs. 1-2), where the Adversary (ha-satan) was granted permission to afflict Job. The "bridle cast off" suggests that supernatural restraint over Job's tormentors has been divinely removed.

verses.flow: On my right hand the rabble rises; they push away my feet; and they cast up against me their ways of destruction.
They break up my path; they promote my calamity; no one hinders them.
As through a wide breach they come; amid the crash they roll themselves in.
Terrors are turned against me; my honor (nadibah) is driven away as by the wind, and my prosperity passes away like a cloud.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Honor" (nadibah, H5071) refers to that which is noble or princely — related to nadib, a willing or generous noble. Job's lament that his "nadibah" vanishes like wind reflects the complete dissolution of his covenantal dignity. "Prosperity" (yeshu'ah) is the same root as "salvation/deliverance" (yeshua), suggesting that Job's wellbeing and his sense of divine rescue have simultaneously evaporated.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "My soul (nephesh) is poured out" (tishaphek alai) mirrors the language of Lamentations 2:12 and anticipates the suffering-servant imagery of Isaiah 53:12 ("he poured out his soul to death"). TYPOLOGY: Job's anguish here functions as a type of the Messiah's desolation — the righteous sufferer, forsaken and poured out, who will ultimately be vindicated. (Isa 53:12; Ps 22:14)

verses.flow: I cry out to you, but you do not answer me; I stand, but you only look upon me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse stands at the spiritual apex of Job's lament — direct address to YHWH with no answer. The Hebrew verb "ta'aneni" (you answer me) followed by its negation is a brutal contrast. Job does not lose his orientation toward God even in desolation; he continues to cry out and to stand before YHWH, which itself is an act of covenantal fidelity. Compare the cry of Ps 22:2 and its NT fulfillment (Matt 27:46).

THEOLOGICAL: The acknowledgment that YHWH brings him to death (tamut) does not preclude Job's earlier confession of resurrection hope (19:25-27), but here the darkness is unrelieved.

verses.flow: And my lyre (kinnor) is turned to mourning, and my pipe (ugab) to the voice of those who weep.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The chapter closes with an inclusio of reversal: just as ch. 29 ended with images of honor, authority, and comfort, ch. 30 ends with instruments of joy (kinnor, the harp/lyre, H3658; ugab, the pipe or flute, H5748) converted to mourning. The kinnor was the instrument of David, of worship, of the Psalter. Its silencing under grief is a profound image of the collapse of the worshipping self. TYPOLOGY: The overturning of the kinnor anticipates Ps 137:2, where Israel hangs its harps on willows in exile — communal desolation echoed in personal grief.

verses.flow: Then the anger of Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, burned — against Job his anger burned, because he had declared himself righteous before God (Elohim).
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "burned" (charah, Strong's H2734) is used twice in this verse for rhetorical emphasis — Elihu's anger is directed both at Job and at the friends, though for opposite reasons. The name "Barachel" means "God blesses," and "Buz" was a son of Nahor, Abraham's brother (Gen 22:21), locating Elihu within the broader Semitic world rather than Israel proper. THEOLOGICAL: Elihu's charge that Job "declared himself righteous before God (Elohim)" echoes the legal idiom of the divine court; his intervention reframes the dialogue as a judicial proceeding before the heavenly assembly.

verses.flow: And against his three friends his anger burned, because they had found no answer, and yet had declared Job guilty.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Masoretic scribal tradition (Tikkun Sopherim) notes that this verse was altered by the scribes — the original reading may have been that the friends "declared God guilty" rather than "declared Job guilty," but the scribes changed it out of reverence. This is one of eighteen scribal emendations known in the Hebrew Bible, preserving sensitivity toward attributing guilt to God.

verses.flow: And Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said: "I am young in days, and you are aged; therefore I held back and feared to declare to you my knowledge (da'at).
I said, 'Let days speak, and let the multitude of years make wisdom (chokmah) known.'
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokmah" (Strong's H2451) denotes practical wisdom and skill, not merely intellectual knowledge. The assumption that age confers wisdom reflects standard ancient Near Eastern wisdom tradition, as seen also in Egyptian instruction literature (e.g., the Instruction of Amenemope). Elihu's subsequent argument subverts this convention.

verses.flow: But truly it is the spirit (ruach) in man, and the breath (neshamah) of the Almighty (Shaddai), that gives them understanding.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruach" (Strong's H7307) carries the range of breath/wind/spirit, and "neshamah" (Strong's H5397) is the divine breath of life breathed into Adam (Gen 2:7). Together these terms elevate Elihu's claim: understanding comes not from accumulated years but from the divine endowment given to every human being. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a significant anthropological statement — wisdom is democratized through the imago Dei and the presence of God's breath in every person, anticipating the New Covenant promise of the Spirit poured out on all flesh (Joel 2:28; Acts 2:17).

verses.flow: The great ones are not wise, nor do the elders understand justice (mishpat).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mishpat" (Strong's H4941) encompasses justice, judgment, and legal ordinance. The claim that elders do not necessarily understand mishpat is a bold challenge to the authority structure of ancient Near Eastern wisdom councils, where age was the primary credential for judicial authority.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Therefore I say, hear me; I also will declare my knowledge.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Behold, I waited for your words; I gave ear to your reasons, while you searched out what to say.
And I gave attention to you, and behold, there was none among you who refuted Job or who answered his words.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Beware lest you say, 'We have found wisdom (chokmah); God (El) drives him away, not man.'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Elihu warns the friends against claiming that God alone can refute Job and that human argument has failed. This anticipates the divine speeches in chapters 38-41, where YHWH indeed speaks directly. Elihu's warning may signal his role as a transitional figure preparing the way for the theophany.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Now he has not directed his words against me, and I will not answer him with your words.
They are dismayed; they answer no more; words have departed from them.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I have waited — for they do not speak, for they stand still and answer no more.
I also will answer my part; I also will declare my knowledge.

verses.flow: For I am full of words; the spirit (ruach) within me constrains me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruach" again appears here, functioning now as prophetic compulsion — the spirit within Elihu presses him to speak. This language is closely parallel to prophetic inspiration formulae used for the classical prophets (cf. Jer 20:9; Amos 3:8), suggesting Elihu understands himself as something of a prophetically inspired mediator. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The idea of being compelled by the spirit (ruach) to speak connects to the heavenly council's dispatch of prophetic messengers; Elihu may be positioning himself as a messenger from the divine assembly to adjudicate the dispute.

verses.flow: Behold, my belly is like wine that is not opened; like new wineskins, it is ready to burst.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of unfermented wine in sealed skins ready to burst is a vivid ANE metaphor for inner pressure demanding release. New wineskins were used precisely because fresh wine produces gas during fermentation; old wineskins had already been stretched to capacity and could not expand. Jesus later inverts this image to speak of the incompatibility of the new covenant with old forms (Matt 9:17; Mark 2:22).

verses.flow: I must speak, that I may find relief; I will open my lips and answer.
I will not lift up the face of any man, nor will I give flattering titles to any person.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For I do not know how to give flattering titles; if I did so, my Maker (Oseh) would soon carry me away.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Oseh" (Strong's H6213, participial form of asah, "to make/do") functions here as a divine title — "my Maker." This title for God is used in wisdom literature to ground ethical accountability in creational relation (cf. Job 4:17; Ps 95:6; Prov 14:31). The fear of one's Maker as the ultimate check on flattery and partiality reflects a theology of divine judgment that transcends human social hierarchies. THEOLOGICAL: Elihu's closing words in this verse ground his claim to impartiality not in his own virtue but in his creaturely accountability before the Creator — a fitting posture with which to introduce his speeches before the theophany.

verses.flow: But now, hear my words, O Job, and to all my speech give ear.
Behold, I have opened my mouth; my tongue in my palate speaks.
My words are the uprightness of my heart, and what my lips know they speak sincerely.

verses.flow: The Spirit (ruach) of God has made me, and the breath (neshamah) of the Almighty (Shaddai) gives me life.
verses.note: HEBREW: Two distinct terms for divine breath appear: "ruach" (H7307, spirit/wind/breath) and "neshamah" (H5397, breath/vital breath). The pairing echoes Gen 2:7 where YHWH breathes "neshamah" into Adam. Elihu grounds his authority to speak in his direct divine creation, distinguishing himself from Job's other friends by invoking the same creative act that gave Job life. THEOLOGICAL: The dual-breath formula affirms that all human rationality and speech are dependent on ongoing divine sustenance, making Elihu's self-humbling before Job rhetorically significant — both men are creatures of the same Maker.

verses.flow: If you are able, answer me; set your words in order before me, take your stand.
Behold, I am toward God as you are; I too was pinched off from clay.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "pinched off from clay" uses the verb qatsar (H7114) in some readings, but more precisely the underlying Hebrew qarats (H7169) means "to nip off" or "pinch," a potter's term for shaping clay — evoking Gen 2:7 and the image of YHWH as divine potter (Isa 64:8). Elihu deliberately levels the status differential: he is no more elevated before God than Job.

verses.flow: Behold, no dread of me need terrify you, nor shall my hand be heavy upon you.

verses.flow: Behold, He finds occasions against me; He counts me as His enemy.

verses.flow: Behold, in this you are not right — I will answer you, for God is greater than man.

verses.flow: For God speaks in one way, and in two ways, yet man does not perceive it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "once...twice" (achat...shtayim) formula is a Hebrew numerical idiom signaling completeness or multiplicity of divine communication. Elihu's point is that God is never silent; the failure is always on the side of human perception. This directly challenges Job's complaint that God will not answer him, reframing the silence as a hearing problem rather than a divine absence.

verses.flow: In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep (tardemah) falls upon men, in slumberings upon the bed,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tardemah" (H8639) denotes a deep, supernaturally induced sleep — the same word used in Gen 2:21 (Adam's sleep) and Gen 15:12 (Abraham's covenant vision). Its use here signals that Elihu views such dream-communication as a legitimate and weighty mode of divine revelation. HISTORICAL: Dream incubation as divine communication was widespread in the ANE (Mesopotamian "šuttu" dreams, Egyptian dream oracles), but Israel's tradition distinguished YHWH's dream-speech as morally purposive rather than merely predictive.

verses.flow: then He opens the ears of men, and with their chastening (musar) He seals the warning,
to turn man aside from his deed, and to conceal pride from a man;
He keeps back his soul from the Pit (shachat), and his life from crossing over by the sword.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shachat" (H7845) is rendered "Pit" throughout this chapter and carries connotations of the grave, destruction, and the underworld — closely related to Sheol terminology. It appears four times in this chapter (vv. 18, 22, 24, 28), forming a structural refrain around the theme of divine rescue from death. THEOLOGICAL: The repeated "shachat" language frames Elihu's entire speech: God's discipline is not punitive abandonment but redemptive rescue from the ultimate fate.

verses.flow: If there is for him an angel (mal'ak), a messenger, one among a thousand, to declare to man his uprightness,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "angel (mal'ak), a messenger, one among a thousand" is a theologically charged phrase. The heavenly mediator figure described here participates in the divine council's function of intercession and declaration. This mal'ak is not merely a general angel but occupies a specific mediatorial office — declaring a human's uprightness before the heavenly court. TYPOLOGY: The singular intercessory angel "one among a thousand" anticipates the NT portrait of Christ as the one Mediator (1 Tim 2:5), the heavenly advocate who stands in the gap. The phrase may also echo the "satan" figure of Job 1-2 but now reversed — an advocate rather than an accuser before the divine council.

verses.flow: and he is gracious to him and says, "Deliver him from going down to the Pit (shachat); I have found a ransom (kofer),"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Kofer" (H3724) is the word for "ransom" or "atonement price" — the sum paid to redeem a forfeited life. It shares a root with "kippur" as in Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement). This is one of the most theologically dense terms in Job. TYPOLOGY: The angelic mediator declaring "I have found a ransom" prefigures the atoning work of the Messiah who provides the kofer that no human can supply for himself (Ps 49:7-9, Mark 10:45). The descent to the Pit being averted by a found ransom is a striking proto-gospel moment within the Wisdom literature.

verses.flow: He entreats God, and He accepts him with favor; and he sees His face with a shout of joy, and He restores to man his righteousness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sequence here — entreaty, divine acceptance, sight of God's face, restoration of righteousness — mirrors the covenantal restoration pattern found in Ps 80, Lam 3, and ultimately in the Messiah's priestly intercession. "Sees His face" (yir'eh panav) uses language elsewhere reserved for throne-room access, implying full covenantal restoration rather than mere physical healing. TYPOLOGY: The restoration of "righteousness" (tsedaqah) to the man anticipates justification language in Paul (Rom 4:3-5).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He sings before men and says, "I have sinned and perverted what was right, and it was not repaid to me.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb rendered "sings" (yashur, from shur H7891) literally means "to sing" in a public, declaratory sense. The confession that follows is not private but testimonial — the restored man becomes a witness before the community. This public dimension of restored relationship with YHWH is significant for understanding the social-covenantal function of repentance in Israel.

verses.flow: He has ransomed my soul from going down into the Pit (shachat), and my life shall look upon the light."
Behold, God works all these things — twice, three times — with a man,
to bring back his soul from the Pit (shachat), that he may be lighted with the light of life.
Give heed, O Job, listen to me; be silent, and I will speak.

verses.flow: If you have words to say, answer me; speak, for I desire to justify you.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: If not, listen to me; be silent, and I will teach you wisdom (chokmah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokmah" (H2451) — wisdom — is the culminating term of Elihu's opening address to Job. In Wisdom literature, chokmah is not merely prudential knowledge but the ordering principle of creation (Prov 8), intimately connected to the fear of YHWH (Job 28:28). Elihu's offer to "teach wisdom" is audacious — he positions himself as a wisdom-mediator, though the book ultimately shows that YHWH Himself will answer from the whirlwind (Job 38-41), surpassing all human wisdom-teachers.

verses.flow: And Elihu answered and said:
"Hear my words, O wise men, and give ear to me, O you who know:
for the ear tests words as the palate tastes food.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "tests" (bachan, Strong's H974) refers to the act of examining or assaying, often used of refining metals; here the ear is likened to a crucible testing the quality of spoken words. The parallel with the palate tasting food echoes Job 12:11, suggesting a tradition of wisdom epistemology grounded in discernment of experience.

verses.flow: Let us choose for ourselves what is right; let us know among ourselves what is good.
For Job has said, 'I am righteous, and El has turned aside my justice.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Elihu quotes Job's own prior speech (Job 27:2, 13:18) to frame his rebuttal. The claim "El has turned aside my justice" (sur mishpati) is the crux of the accusation: Job appears to charge God with judicial misconduct, which Elihu treats as an intolerable theological error requiring correction.

verses.flow: In spite of my right I am counted a liar; my wound is incurable, though I am without transgression.'
What man is like Job, who drinks up scoffing like water,

verses.flow: For he has said, 'It profits a man nothing that he should take delight in Elohim.'
Therefore, O men of heart, hear me: far be it from El to do wickedness, and from Shaddai to do wrong.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The double name construction "El...Shaddai" emphasizes the full majesty of the deity. The affirmation that Shaddai cannot "pervert justice" (avath mishpat) is a foundational axiom of retribution theology; Elihu's entire argument rests on the impossibility of divine injustice. Compare Deuteronomy 32:4 and Genesis 18:25.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For the work of a man he repays to him, and according to the way of a man he causes him to find it.
Of a truth, El will not do wickedly, and Shaddai will not pervert justice.
Who gave him charge over the earth, and who laid on him the whole world?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The rhetorical question "Who gave him charge over the earth?" (mi-pakad alav eretz) invokes the motif of divine sovereignty over creation and over lesser cosmic administrators. In the divine council worldview, the question implies that no higher authority delegated governance to YHWH — he is the ultimate sovereign, not merely a regional deity.

verses.flow: If he should set his heart to it, if he should gather to himself his ruach (spirit) and his breath,
verses.note: HEBREW: The term ruach (Strong's H7307) here denotes the animating breath-spirit that El originally granted to humanity (Genesis 2:7). The conditional "if he should gather to himself his ruach" pictures God withdrawing the vital principle he lent at creation, a striking image of creaturely dependence on continuous divine sustenance.

words.g: and ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: all flesh would expire together, and man would return to the dust.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Man would return to the dust" (yashuv al-aphar) echoes the creation-death formula of Genesis 3:19. The corporate mortality of "all flesh" prefigures the Pauline theology of death-in-Adam (Romans 5:12) and the eschatological reversal through resurrection in Christ, who as the last Adam restores what the first Adam forfeited.

verses.flow: If now you have understanding, hear this; give ear to the sound of my words.
Shall even one who hates justice govern? And will you condemn him who is righteous and mighty?
Is it fitting to say to a king, 'Worthless one,' and to nobles, 'Wicked men'?
How much less to him who does not lift up the face of princes, nor regards the rich more than the poor; for they are all the work of his hands.

verses.flow: In a moment they die; at midnight the people are shaken and pass away, and the mighty are taken away without hand.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The mighty are taken away without hand" (lo b'yad) stresses that divine judgment requires no human instrument or visible agent, underscoring YHWH's transcendent sovereignty over rulers. The midnight motif recalls the Exodus plague of the firstborn (Exodus 12:29), linking divine judicial action to historical paradigm.

verses.flow: For his eyes are upon the ways of a man, and he sees all his steps.
There is no darkness and no deep shadow where the workers of iniquity may hide themselves.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "No darkness and no deep shadow" (choshek w'tzalmavet) — the term tzalmavet (shadow of death, Strong's H6757) appears throughout the Psalms (Psalm 23:4) and earlier in Job (3:5; 10:21). Elihu's point is that cosmic darkness provides no refuge from divine omniscience, anticipating Psalm 139:12.

verses.flow: For he has not appointed a time for any man to go before El in judgment.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He shatters the mighty without investigation, and sets others in their place.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of shattering the mighty "without investigation" and setting others in their place resonates with Psalm 82:6-7, where divine council members (elohim) are judged and displaced. Elihu may be invoking a broader pattern of divine council governance in which enthroned powers are toppled and replaced at YHWH's sovereign discretion.

verses.flow: Therefore he takes note of their works; he overturns them in the night, and they are crushed.

verses.flow: In the place of the wicked he strikes them, in a place where people see,
because they turned aside from following him, and had no regard for any of his ways,
so as to cause the cry of the poor to come before him, that he might hear the cry of the afflicted.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cry of the poor (tza'akat aniyyim) reaching the divine ear is a covenant motif appearing throughout the Hebrew Bible (Exodus 3:7; Psalm 72:12). This legal-theological principle — that YHWH functions as supreme appellate judge for the marginalized — provides the ironic counterpoint to Job's own complaint that his cry goes unanswered.

verses.flow: Shall he then repay it according to your terms, because you reject it? For you must choose, and not I; and what you know, speak.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Elihu's challenge "you must choose, and not I" forces Job into a position of moral accountability before the divine judge. This rhetorical strategy anticipates YHWH's own divine speech from the whirlwind (Job 38-41), where God similarly places Job under interrogation rather than answering his accusations directly.

verses.flow: Men of heart will say to me, and the wise man who hears me:

verses.flow: 'Job speaks without knowledge, and his words are without insight.'
My desire is that Job be tested to the end, because his answers are like those of wicked men.
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening word "my desire" translates the Hebrew avivi (or the form translated "my father"), a crux interpretum; some manuscripts and versions suggest "alas for Job" or "would that Job be tested." The request that Job be tested "to the end" (ad-netzach) uses the term netzach (Strong's H5331), meaning perpetuity or endurance, indicating Elihu believes Job's ordeal is not yet sufficient to expose his true character.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: For he adds rebellion to his sin; he claps his hands among us and multiplies his words against El."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "He adds rebellion (pesha, Strong's H6588) to his sin" employs the strongest term for covenant-breaking transgression — pesha denotes willful revolt, not merely inadvertent error. For Elihu, Job's oral argumentation against divine justice has elevated his suffering from passive endurance into active defiance, compounding guilt rather than demonstrating innocence.
