verses.flow: Then Elihu answered and said:
"Do you think this to be right — your judgment — that you say, 'My righteousness is more than God's'?
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "your righteousness is more than God's" (tsidqi me-El) reflects the Hebrew comparative preposition mem (מ), rendered "more than." Elihu charges Job with asserting a kind of moral superiority over the divine, which is the theological crux of his entire third speech. The term tsedaqah (righteousness, H6666) will echo through the later divine speeches.

verses.flow: For you say, 'What advantage is it to you? What profit do I have, more than if I had sinned?'

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: I will answer you with words, and your companions with you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Look toward the heavens and see; behold the clouds — they are higher than you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: If you have sinned, what do you accomplish against Him? And if your transgressions are multiplied, what do you do to Him?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Elihu's argument here anticipates a key theme in divine council theology: God is ontologically self-sufficient and cannot be diminished by human sin nor augmented by human righteousness. This echoes Psalm 16:2 ("my goodness extends not to You") and establishes the asymmetry between creature and Creator that underlies all covenant relationship.

verses.flow: If you are righteous, what do you give to Him? Or what does He receive from your hand?
Your wickedness concerns a man like yourself, and your righteousness a son of man.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "son of man" (ben-adam, H1121 + H120) is the standard Hebrew idiom for a mortal human being, emphasizing frailty and creatureliness. Elihu's point is that righteousness and wickedness play out within the horizontal sphere of human community — they do not alter God's being. This phrase carries rich typological resonance given its later messianic use in Daniel 7:13.

verses.flow: From the multitude of oppressions they cry out; they cry for help because of the arm of the mighty.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "multitude of oppressions" (rob ashukkim) reflects the social reality of ancient Near Eastern life where the poor cried out under the burden of powerful overlords. The book of Job is set in the patriarchal era and shares vocabulary with Ugaritic literature. The cry (ze'aqah, H2201) for help from oppressors is the same root used of Israel's cry in Egypt (Exod 2:23).

verses.flow: But none says, 'Where is God my Maker, who gives songs (zemiroth) in the night,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "God my Maker" (Elohai Osai) is a plural participle — literally "my Makers" — a grammatically plural form applied to the singular God, consistent with the Hebrew use of plural majesty (cf. Gen 1:1, Eccl 12:1). The "songs (zemiroth) in the night" may evoke the divine gift of praise and hope even in darkness — a striking contrast to Job's lament (cf. Ps 42:8; Acts 16:25).

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth, and makes us wiser than the birds of the heavens?'
There they cry out, but He does not answer, because of the pride of evil men.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The failure of God to answer is attributed not to divine absence but to human "pride" (ge'on, H1347) — the arrogance of the wicked that disqualifies the cry. This theme runs through Psalm 82, where YHWH judges the divine assembly for failing to deliver the poor. The implication is that corrupt spiritual conditions, whether in human leaders or heavenly beings, corrupt the channel of justice.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Surely God does not hear an empty cry, nor does the Almighty (Shaddai) regard it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shaddai" (H7706) is one of the oldest divine epithets in the Hebrew Bible, commonly rendered "the Almighty." Its etymology is debated — possibly related to shadad (to overpower) or Akkadian shadu (mountain). Its use here is deliberate: Elihu invokes God's overwhelming power as the basis for His sovereign freedom to answer or withhold answer. The term "empty cry" (shav, H7723) also means "vanity" or "falsehood," implying the cry lacks integrity.

verses.flow: How much less when you say you do not perceive Him — the case is before Him, and you wait for Him.
And now, because His anger has not visited, and He does not greatly know transgression,
therefore Job opens his mouth in vanity; he multiplies words without knowledge."
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Elihu's closing indictment — that Job "multiplies words without knowledge" — is striking because YHWH uses nearly identical language in 38:2, directing the same charge at Job at the opening of the divine speeches. This verbal echo either vindicates Elihu's diagnosis or sets up an ironic contrast when God Himself addresses Job directly, bypassing Elihu entirely. Elihu receives no divine rebuke in the epilogue (42:7-9), which has generated significant interpretive debate.

verses.flow: I will carry my knowledge from afar, and to my Maker I will ascribe righteousness.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "carry my knowledge from afar" (esa de'i lemerachok) uses the verb nasa (H5375, to lift, carry) with de'i, a rare Aramaic-tinged word for knowledge. Elihu's claim to speak for God echoes prophetic commissioning language, though the NT makes clear that only Christ perfectly mediates divine wisdom (Col 2:3). THEOLOGICAL: Elihu positions himself as a spokesman of divine righteousness (tsedaqah, H6664), setting up his theodicy — the vindication of God's justice in allowing suffering.

verses.flow: For truly my words are not false; one perfect in knowledge is with you.
Behold, God is mighty, and he does not despise any; he is mighty in strength of understanding.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verse establishes two divine attributes in tension and unity: God's power (koach, H3581) and his understanding (lev, lit. "heart," here meaning intellect and wisdom). This refutes Job's implication that God acts arbitrarily. HEBREW: "Does not despise any" translates lo yim'as, from the root ma'as (H3988, to reject, despise), emphasizing God's impartiality — a central theme in wisdom literature.

verses.flow: He does not preserve the life of the wicked, but the justice (mishpat) of the afflicted ones he gives.
verses.note: HEBREW: Mishpat (H4941) carries the full weight of covenant legal justice — not merely a verdict but the enactment of right order. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between the wicked (rasha') and the afflicted (aniyim) is foundational to Elihu's argument: God is not indifferent to suffering but uses it as a refining instrument. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the one who "gives justice to the afflicted" fulfills this divine role perfectly (Isa 61:1; Luke 4:18).

verses.flow: Will he esteem your cry for help? — not in distress, nor any of the forces of strength.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "God is exalted in his power" (El yisgeh bekocho) — this declaration pivots the speech from theodicy to doxology. Elihu begins a sustained hymn to divine sovereignty in creation (vv. 22-33), asserting that no human teacher rivals God. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the divine Teacher (Rabbi, Didaskalos) fulfills this uniquely — "no one has ever spoken like this man" (John 7:46); he is Wisdom incarnate (1 Cor 1:24).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "We do not know him" (lo neda'ennu) is a remarkable admission embedded in a speech of great confidence. Elihu acknowledges the limits of creaturely knowledge before the infinite God. "The number of his years is unsearchable" asserts divine eternality — God exists outside the measurement of time. HEBREW: The root chaqar (H2714, to search out, investigate) appears in its negative form, echoing Job's own earlier complaints that God is beyond investigation (Job 9:11).

The Masoretic vocalization supports "mist" or "vapor." HISTORICAL: Ancient Near Eastern cosmologies also recognized the role of heavenly waters and mist in precipitation, though they attributed it to competing deities; Elihu here ascribes the entire process to the one sovereign Creator God.

The "spreadings of the clouds" as God's cosmic canopy suggests the storm theophany that culminates in YHWH's own speech in chapters 38-41. THEOLOGICAL: This is not mere meteorology — it is theological cosmology: the storm is the chariot-throne of the sovereign God.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God's governance of nations through weather phenomena ("by these he judges the peoples") reflects the biblical theology of divine sovereignty over history and nature simultaneously. The same storm that judges also provides food — a paradox of divine mercy and judgment held in tension. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This governing of peoples through cosmic forces connects to the framework of Deut 32:8-9, where divine oversight of nations involves both heavenly and earthly dimensions.

verses.note: HEBREW: The final verse is notoriously difficult in the MT. The LXX and Syriac versions offer variant readings. THEOLOGICAL: Whether the witnesses to God's thunderous announcement are mankind or cattle, the point is doxological: all creation — animate and inanimate — testifies to the majesty of the Creator, anticipating YHWH's own self-disclosure in the whirlwind (Job 38:1).

Elihu's physical reaction anticipates the approach of YHWH's storm-speech in chapters 38-41, signaling that the climax of the book is imminent. The phrase "leaps from its place" (yitten miqomo) is strikingly visceral, suggesting the heart is nearly dislodged by awe.

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "El" (H410) is used here rather than YHWH, a pattern consistent throughout the Elihu speeches and the divine council poetic tradition. El is the supreme deity of the Canaanite pantheon, but the biblical author deploys the name to assert that Israel's God alone occupies that supreme position. The phrase "great things which we cannot comprehend" (gedolot lo' neda) anticipates YHWH's own rhetorical questions in Job 38-39.

Some interpreters see here a reference to divine providence overruling human autonomy. The purpose clause "that all men may know it" (lada'at) grounds natural phenomena in revelatory intent.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The image of "a cast metal mirror" (re'i mutsaq) reflects the ancient practice of making mirrors from polished bronze or copper. Such mirrors were prized possessions in the ancient Near East, notable for their hardness and reflective clarity. The comparison to the sky emphasizes both its solidity and luminosity from the divine perspective, echoing the "firmament" (raqia) of Genesis 1:6-8.

verses.flow: And now, they do not look on the light when it is bright in the skies, after a wind has passed and cleared them.
Out of the north comes golden splendor; around Eloah is awesome majesty.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Out of the north comes golden splendor" (mitstaphon zahav yeta) is highly significant theologically. In Canaanite cosmology, Mount Zaphon (the north) was the dwelling place of Baal and the divine assembly. The biblical author here co-opts this language to assert that true divine glory and majesty belong exclusively to Eloah, the God of Israel. This verse anticipates the theophany of YHWH in chapter 38. Compare (Isa 14:13) and (Ps 48:2).

verses.flow: The Almighty (Shaddai) — we cannot find him; he is great in power; justice and great righteousness he will not violate.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine name "Shaddai" (H7706, El Shaddai, "God Almighty" or possibly "God of the Mountain") appears prominently in Job more than in any other biblical book, reflecting its antiquity and patriarchal connections (Exod 6:3). Elihu's climactic confession — that Shaddai is beyond finding out, yet will not pervert justice — is the theological summit of his speech and sets the stage for the divine answer from the whirlwind.

verses.flow: Therefore men fear him; he does not regard any who are wise in their own heart.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The closing statement that God "does not regard any who are wise in their own heart" anticipates the NT theme of divine reversal of human wisdom (1 Cor 1:19-25). Job's comforters, in their self-assured theological system, ironically exemplify those wise in their own eyes. Only the one who fears God — as Job ultimately does — stands rightly before him. Compare (Prov 3:7) and (Isa 5:21).

verses.flow: Then YHWH answered Job out of the whirlwind and said:
verses.note: HEBREW: The term rendered "whirlwind" is se'arah (H5591), a violent storm-wind, the same phenomenon associated with Elijah's translation (2 Kgs 2:1) and Ezekiel's throne-chariot vision (Ezek 1:4). YHWH's speaking from the storm is a classic theophany motif, asserting divine sovereignty over the very forces of chaos Job has been lamenting. THEOLOGICAL: God does not answer Job's questions directly but responds with counter-questions, shifting the entire epistemic frame from "why do I suffer?" to "who are you before the Creator?"

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "darkens counsel" (machshik etzah) implies that Job's speeches, however sincere, have obscured — not illuminated — the true wisdom governing creation. This is not a rebuke of Job's honesty but of his epistemic overreach. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the Wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24) is the one who never darkens counsel; he is the Logos through whom all creation's order is established (John 1:3).

STRUCTURE: This verse functions as a formal rhetorical introduction to the divine speech, establishing the question-and-answer framework that structures chapters 38–41.

verses.flow: Their young ones become strong; they grow up in the open field; they go out and do not return to them.
Who has sent out the wild donkey free, and who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey—
verses.note: HEBREW: Two distinct terms appear: "wild donkey" (pere', H6501) and "swift donkey" ('arod, H6171), possibly distinguishing species or the same animal described by two names. "Bonds" (moserot, H4147) — the same word used for the cords of the Pleiades (38:31), connecting the themes of cosmic freedom YHWH alone grants. THEOLOGICAL: Freedom in creation is a divine prerogative; YHWH grants wildness as a theological statement about his sovereign ordering of creation.

verses.flow: to whom I have given the wilderness as his home, and the salt flats as his dwelling place?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The salt flats (melo'ach, H4414, literally "salt place") describe the barren terrain of the Dead Sea region or arid desert lowlands, considered uninhabitable by humans but appointed by YHWH as the wild donkey's dwelling. The assignment of specific habitats reflects ANE divine-ordering cosmology where the high god assigns each creature its domain.

verses.flow: He scorns the tumult of the city; the shouts of the driver he does not hear.

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: He ranges the mountains as his pasture, and he searches after every green thing.

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Is the wild ox willing to serve you? Will he lodge at your feeding trough at night?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wild ox" (re'em, H7214) — a powerful, now-extinct aurochs (Bos primigenius), attested in Akkadian as rimu, depicted in Mesopotamian royal iconography as a symbol of untamable might. It is invoked elsewhere as a symbol of YHWH's own strength (Num 23:22) and of the Davidic king's power (Ps 92:10). THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical irony is pointed — the creature that symbolizes divine power cannot be tamed by Job, underscoring the gulf between Creator and creature.

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Can you bind the wild ox in the furrow with his rope, or will he harrow the valleys after you?

verses.flow: Will you depend on him because his strength is great, and will you leave to him your labor?

words.m: particle + verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Can you trust him to bring home your grain, and to gather it to your threshing floor?
The wing of the ostrich waves joyously—but is it a pinion of lovingkindness (hesed), or of a stork?
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse is notoriously difficult in the MT. "Waves joyously" (ra'ananah, possibly from H7442) and "pinion of lovingkindness (hesed)" — hesed (H2617) here may be ironic, contrasting the ostrich's beautiful wing with its apparent lack of maternal lovingkindness. The stork (chasidah, H2624) is literally "the loyal/steadfast one" (from hesed), making the contrast stark: the stork embodies hesed in its care; the ostrich does not.

verses.flow: For she abandons her eggs to the earth and lets them be warmed on the ground,

verses.flow: and she forgets that a foot may crush them, and that the wild beast may trample them.
She deals cruelly with her young, as if they were not hers; her labor is in vain, without fear,

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: for God has made her forget wisdom, and he has not given her a share in understanding.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a remarkable statement on divine sovereignty in the allocation of wisdom. God is explicitly said to have withheld wisdom from the ostrich — a direct challenge to any assumption that all creatures are equally endowed with understanding. This stands in tension with Job's implicit demand that God operate by a rational, equitable system accessible to human judgment. (Rom 9:20-21)

verses.flow: When she rouses herself to flee, she laughs at the horse and his rider.

verses.flow: Do you give the horse his might? Do you clothe his neck with a mane (ra'amah)?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mane" (ra'amah, H7483) — a hapax legomenon (occurring only here), derived from a root meaning "to tremble" or "to thunder," suggesting the flowing mane as a terrifying, thundering display of power. The horse's neck-clothing with this trembling glory is presented as YHWH's creative act, not the result of breeding or training.

words.m: particle + verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Do you make him leap like the locust? The majesty of his snorting is terror.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The warhorse was among the most expensive and status-laden possessions in the ANE world. Horses were introduced into Canaan ca. 2000 BCE and became central to Egyptian and Assyrian military power. The imagery here reflects firsthand familiarity with military culture. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH presents the warhorse as his own handiwork — the fearsome creature that kings depend on for conquest was fashioned by the one addressing Job from the whirlwind.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He paws in the valley and exults in his strength; he goes out to meet the weapons.
He laughs at fear and is not dismayed; he does not turn back from the sword.
Against him rattles the quiver, the flashing spear and the javelin.
With fierceness and rage he swallows the ground; he cannot stand still at the sound of the trumpet.
When the trumpet sounds, he says, "Aha!" He smells the battle from afar, the thunder of the captains and the war cry.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The warhorse who rushes toward the trumpet anticipating battle has been read typologically as a shadow of the divine warrior motif — the celestial horseman of Revelation who leads the armies of heaven (Rev 19:11-14). The one who "smells the battle from afar" is ultimately under YHWH's creative command, pointing to a cosmos ordered for divine purposes beyond human comprehension.

verses.flow: Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars and spreads his wings toward the south?
Is it at your command that the eagle mounts up and makes his nest on high?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The eagle mounting to its nest "on high" (marom, H4791) echoes the cosmic heights reserved for divine beings in Hebrew cosmology. While the immediate reference is naturalistic, "on high" in the Psalms and prophets frequently describes the heavenly realm (Ps 93:4; Isa 14:14). The nesting of the eagle in the heights mirrors the divine council's habitation of the heavenly realm, both being domains inaccessible to Job. TYPOLOGY: The eagle soaring at YHWH's command anticipates the NT image of those who "mount up with wings like eagles" (Isa 40:31), strength renewed by trust in YHWH alone.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpolel imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: On the rock he dwells and makes his home, on the rocky crag and the stronghold.
From there he spies out the prey; his eyes behold it from afar.

verses.flow: His young ones suck up blood, and where the slain are, there he is.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The closing image — young eagles drinking blood where the slain lie — is directly quoted by Jesus in his eschatological discourse (Matt 24:28; Luke 17:37): "Where the corpse is, there the vultures will gather." Jesus invokes this creational reality as a sign of coming judgment, connecting YHWH's speech from the whirlwind to the Son's own proclamation of cosmic judgment. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter ends not with comfort but with raw creaturely violence — a reminder that the world YHWH governs is not a tame or sentimental place, and that his wisdom encompasses dimensions of creation that exceed human moral categories.

verses.flow: And YHWH answered Job out of the whirlwind and said:
verses.note: HEBREW: The chapter opens with YHWH's second speech from the whirlwind (se'arah, H5591), the same theophanic storm vehicle as in chapter 38. The verb translated "answered" ('anah, H6030) implies a formal, authoritative response, not merely a reply to Job's words but a divine pronouncement bearing covenantal weight.

words.m: verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: "Shall a fault-finder contend with the Almighty? He who argues with God, let him answer it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The word translated "fault-finder" (rib, H7378) is a legal covenant-lawsuit term used throughout the prophets (Mic 6:2; Hos 4:1). YHWH frames the entire exchange as a juridical contest, placing Job in the position of a plaintiff against the Almighty — an audacious stance that the speech now systematically dismantles.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Job answered YHWH and said:

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: "Behold, I am of small account (qalal); what shall I answer You? I lay my hand upon my mouth.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qalal" (H7043) means to be slight, trifling, or of small weight — the opposite of "glory" or "honor." Job's placing his hand upon his mouth (a gesture of awed silence) is a Near Eastern idiom of submission and wonder, seen also in Job 21:5 and Prov 30:32. His silence is not coerced but reverent.

verses.flow: Once I have spoken, and I will not answer; even twice, but I will proceed no further."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then YHWH answered Job out of the whirlwind and said:

verses.flow: "Gird up your loins now like a man; I will question you, and you make known to Me.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The summons to "gird up your loins like a man (geber, H1397)" echoes the language applied to Jeremiah's commissioning (Jer 1:17) and anticipates the language of spiritual readiness in the New Testament (1 Pet 1:13; Eph 6:14). The word "geber" specifically denotes a warrior-man, implying that this interrogation is a contest of cosmic stature.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Will you even put My justice (mishpat) at naught? Will you condemn Me that you may be in the right?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's question cuts to the theological core of the book: "Will you put My mishpat (justice, H4941) at naught?" This is the same word used in Job's own complaints that justice was denied him (Job 27:2). YHWH here reveals that Job's lament, however understandable, carried an implicit verdict against divine governance — a charge YHWH now addresses not with argument but with display of sovereign power.

words.g: and + in/with a voice or sound

verses.flow: Or do you have an arm like God, and can you thunder with a voice like His?
Adorn yourself now with majesty (ga'on) and excellency, and clothe yourself with glory (hod) and splendor (hadar).
verses.note: HEBREW: Four terms for divine splendor are stacked here: ga'on (H1347, majesty/pride in its positive divine sense), gaboah (excellency/height), hod (H1935, splendor/majesty, often associated with royal or divine radiance), and hadar (H1926, ornamental beauty/honor). This fourfold array appears together in royal enthronement contexts (Ps 104:1; 96:6), suggesting YHWH is here clothed in the attributes of the cosmic King.

verses.flow: Pour out the overflowings of your anger, and look upon everyone who is proud, and bring him low.

verses.flow: Look upon everyone who is proud; abase him, and tread down the wicked where they stand.

verses.flow: Hide them in the dust together; bind their faces in the hidden place.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Then I also will acknowledge to you that your own right hand can save you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 14 is the hinge of the second divine speech. YHWH's conditional — "then I also will acknowledge to you that your own right hand can save you" — is a devastating irony. Salvation by one's own right hand is the prerogative of God alone (Ps 44:3; Isa 63:5). The rhetorical point is that if Job cannot do what YHWH alone can do, Job cannot serve as his own judge. This anticipates the New Testament's answer: it is Messiah's right hand that saves (Acts 5:31).

verses.flow: Behold now Behemoth (Behemoth), which I made as I made you; he eats grass like an ox.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Behemoth (a Hebrew intensive plural of behemah, H929, "beast") has been interpreted as hippopotamus, elephant, or a mythological chaos creature. In Second Temple tradition and the broader ANE worldview, Behemoth and Leviathan (ch. 41) represent the great cosmic monsters subdued by the Creator at the foundation of the world (cf. 1 En. 60:7-9; 4 Ezra 6:49-52; Ps 74:13-14). Their inclusion here places Job's suffering within a cosmic theater where YHWH alone commands the primordial powers.

verses.flow: Behold now, his strength is in his loins, and his power (ono) in the muscles of his belly.
He makes his tail stiff like a cedar; the sinews of his thighs are knit together.
verses.note: HEBREW: "He makes his tail stiff like a cedar" — the verb chaphets (H2654, to delight in, to make firm) in the Hiphil suggests deliberate, energetic movement. The cedar (erez, H730) was the tallest, most powerful tree in the ancient Levantine world, used as a metaphor for royal power and permanence (Ps 92:12; Ezek 31:3). The anatomical comparison underlines Behemoth's extraordinary physical design.

verses.flow: His bones are tubes of bronze; his limbs are like bars of iron.

words.m: particle + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: He is the first (re'shit) of the works of God; let Him who made him bring near His sword.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Re'shit (H7225) of the works of God" — this term, also the first word of Genesis ("In the beginning"), carries the sense of "first-rank" or "chief" of created things. This is a staggering theological claim: Behemoth is presented as the premier creature, the masterwork of creation, and yet YHWH alone commands the sword against it. The implication is that no created power, however primordially great, stands beyond divine sovereignty. Compare the use of re'shit in Prov 8:22 for Wisdom's preexistent role.

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: For the mountains yield food (bul) for him, and all the wild beasts of the field play there.
Under the lotus plants he lies, in the shelter of the reeds and in the marsh.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: The lotus trees cover him for shade; the willows of the brook surround him.
Behold, if the river is violent, he does not tremble; he is confident though the Jordan rushes against his mouth.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The reference to "the Jordan" (Yarden, H3383) is one of the few geographically specific references in the entire book of Job, which is otherwise set in the land of Uz. Some scholars see this as a later editorial addition; others note that the Jordan's seasonal flooding was proverbially violent (Jer 12:5; 49:19), making it a culturally recognized image of overwhelming force — exactly the kind of torrent that would terrify ordinary creatures but leaves Behemoth unmoved.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Can one take him by his eyes, or pierce his nose with a snare?"
verses.note: HEBREW: The closing rhetorical question, "Can one take him by his eyes?" — literally "in his eyes" (be'einayw) — may mean while he is watching, i.e., openly. The snare (moqesh, H4170) is a standard hunting trap. The point is that Behemoth cannot be captured by any human stratagem, open or covert. Only YHWH can "bring near His sword" to him (v. 19). The chapter ends with a question that hangs in the air, answered implicitly by God alone.

verses.flow: Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook, or press down his tongue with a cord?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Leviathan" (livyatan, Strong's H3882) derives from a root meaning "to twist" or "to coil," evoking a great sea serpent. In the ANE cosmological framework, Leviathan parallels the Ugaritic chaos-dragon Lôtan, the seven-headed serpent slain by Baal. YHWH's rhetorical questions assert His absolute sovereign mastery over this creature that terrifies all mortals, anchoring the theophanic argument begun in chapter 38.

verses.flow: Can you put a rope in his nose, or pierce his jaw with a hook?

verses.flow: Will he make many supplications to you? Will he speak soft words to you?
Will he make a covenant with you? Will you take him as a servant forever?
Will you play with him as with a bird, or will you bind him for your maidens?
Will traders bargain over him? Will they divide him among the merchants?
Can you fill his skin with harpoons, or his head with fishing spears?
Lay your hand upon him; remember the battle — you will not do it again!
Behold, the hope of a man is disappointed; even at the sight of him one is cast down.
No one is so fierce that he dares to arouse him; who then is he who can stand before Me?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pivot of the entire Leviathan discourse falls here: if no mortal dares rouse Leviathan, how much less can any creature stand before the Creator Himself? YHWH's rhetorical logic moves from creature to Creator, silencing Job's demands for a legal hearing. This verse echoes the divine council's understanding that YHWH alone is incomparable among all beings (Ps 89:6-8).

verses.flow: Who has first given to Me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under all the heavens is Mine.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH asserts absolute ontological priority — no creature has prior claim upon the Creator. This verse is the theological heart of Job's answer: all things subsist in YHWH's gift, making any human legal complaint against Him philosophically incoherent. Paul alludes to this logic in Romans 11:35 ("Or who has given a gift to him that he might be repaid?"), applying it to the mystery of Israel's salvation.

verses.flow: I will not keep silence concerning his limbs, or his mighty strength, or his well-formed frame.
Who can strip off his outer garment? Who can penetrate his double coat of mail?

words.g: in/with the back of the neck

verses.flow: Who can open the doors of his face? Around his teeth is terror.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The doors of his face" (dalthoth panayv) is a striking idiom for the jaws or muzzle of Leviathan, treating the creature's mouth as a fortified gateway. "Terror" (eimah, Strong's H367) denotes the visceral dread that accompanies the presence of something overwhelmingly dangerous, the same term used of divine terror before holy war (Gen 15:12, Exod 23:27).

verses.flow: His back is made of rows of shields, shut up closely as with a tight seal.
One is so near to another that no air can come between them.

words.g: from an elevation or leprous scab

verses.flow: They are joined one to another; they clasp each other and cannot be separated.
His sneezings flash forth light, and his eyes are like the eyelids of the dawn.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ancient Near Eastern dragon iconography consistently depicts chaos creatures with luminous eyes and fire-breathing mouths. Mesopotamian depictions of the mushussu dragon and Egyptian Apophis show similar features. The description here is not merely poetic hyperbole but draws on a shared cosmological vocabulary that the original audience would have recognized as marking Leviathan as the supreme chaos creature.

verses.flow: Out of his mouth go burning torches; sparks of fire leap forth.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The fire-breathing description of Leviathan resonates with the portrayal of the great dragon (ho drakon ho megas) in Revelation 12-13, who wages war against the people of God. The ultimate defeat of this Leviathan-figure by the Lamb (Rev 19-20) fulfills the Isaianic promise of YHWH's eschatological slaying of Leviathan (Isa 27:1), the serpent of old.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Out of his nostrils smoke goes forth, as from a boiling pot and burning rushes.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: His breath sets coals ablaze, and a flame goes forth from his mouth.
In his neck strength lodges, and dismay dances before him.

words.g: a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: The folds of his flesh are joined together, firm upon him, immovable.
His heart is hard as stone, yes, hard as a lower millstone.
When he raises himself up, the mighty are afraid; at the crashing they are beside themselves.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The mighty" (elim, Strong's H410 pl.) can be rendered "the gods" or "divine beings," suggesting that even members of the heavenly host are terrified at Leviathan's rising. This reading would place Leviathan within the divine council's cosmological frame as a chaos power that threatens the order YHWH established, only fully subdued under YHWH's direct sovereignty (cf. Ps 89:9-10).

verses.flow: Though the sword reaches him, it cannot avail; nor the spear, the dart, or the javelin.

conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Job answered YHWH and said:
"I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Job's confession opens with an affirmation of divine omnipotence using the root 'yada' (to know, H3045), signaling a genuine epistemic shift. The phrase "no purpose of yours can be thwarted" (lo' yibbatser) uses the same root as the Tower of Babel account (Gen 11:6), implying that no human scheme or cosmic force can restrain YHWH's sovereign will. This is the climactic theological turning point of the entire book.

verses.flow: Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have declared what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.
verses.note: HEBREW: Job quotes back YHWH's own challenge from 38:2 verbatim — "Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge?" — a profound rhetorical act of self-indictment. The term "wonderful" (nipla'ot, H6381) refers to acts or realities beyond human comprehension, the same word used of YHWH's mighty deeds in Exodus and Psalms (Ps 77:11). Job does not retract his anguish but submits his intellect to divine mystery.

verses.flow: Hear, and I will speak; I will question you, and you make known to me.

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: By the hearing of the ear I had heard of you, but now my eye has seen you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between "hearing of the ear" (shema' ozen) and "my eye has seen you" ('ein ra'atka) is the experiential climax of the book. Job has moved from secondhand theological knowledge to direct theophanic encounter. This verse has profound implications for how the Hebrew tradition understands the purpose of suffering — not primarily as punishment, but as the crucible of genuine encounter with the living God. Cf. (Isa 6:5); (Rev 1:17).

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Therefore I despise myself, and I repent in dust and ashes."
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb translated "despise myself" (em'as, H3988) lacks an explicit object in Hebrew, making it ambiguous — it may mean "I reject [my words]" or "I dissolve [in humility]" rather than self-loathing. "Repent" (nachamti, H5162) carries the nuance of being comforted or consoled as well as changing one's mind; some scholars render it "I am comforted." The phrase "dust and ashes" ('afar va'efer) echoes Abraham's self-description before YHWH in Gen 18:27, marking covenant humility before the divine presence.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And it came to pass after YHWH had spoken these words to Job, YHWH said to Eliphaz the Temanite: "My wrath is kindled against you and against your two friends, for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's verdict is striking: Job, who complained and demanded answers, is declared to have spoken "what is right" (nachon, H3559 — established, firm, true), while the friends who defended divine orthodoxy are condemned. This overturns simplistic retributive theology. The repetition of "my servant Job" ('avdi Iyov) four times in vv. 7-8 echoes the divine introduction in 1:8, forming a literary bracket and vindicating Job's covenant status. Cf. (Jas 5:11).

verses.flow: Now therefore take for yourselves seven bulls and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and offer up for yourselves a burnt offering (olah); and my servant Job shall pray for you, for I will accept his prayer, that I deal not with you according to your folly. For you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The requirement of seven bulls and seven rams as a burnt offering (olah, H5930 — that which ascends) for the friends' atonement is a priestly act of substitutionary intercession. That Job — not a Levitical priest — functions as intercessor anticipates the priestly role of the Messiah who intercedes for those who have wronged him (Isa 53:12); (Heb 7:25). The number seven signals completeness and covenantal fullness in ANE sacrificial practice.

verses.flow: So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Zophar the Naamathite went and did as YHWH had told them, and YHWH accepted Job's prayer.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite represent wisdom traditions from Edom (Teman), northern Arabia (Shuah), and possibly Syria or Transjordan (Naamah). Their compliance with YHWH's instruction demonstrates that even non-Israelite sages are accountable to YHWH's direct revelation. The acceptance of Job's intercessory prayer (vayissa' YHWH et-penei Iyov) uses the idiom "to lift the face," meaning to look upon with favor (H5375 + H6440).

verses.flow: And YHWH restored the fortunes of Job when he prayed for his friends, and YHWH gave Job twice as much as he had before.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Restored the fortunes" (shav YHWH et-shevut) is a technical covenantal phrase (H7622) used frequently in the prophets for the restoration of Israel from exile (Jer 30:18; Amos 9:14). Its application here to an individual suggests Job's story functions as a microcosm of Israel's exile-and-restoration pattern. The doubling of possessions recalls the double restitution required in the Torah for theft (Exod 22:4), implying YHWH acknowledges the genuine injustice of Job's suffering.

verses.flow: Then came to him all his brothers and all his sisters and all who had known him before, and they ate bread with him in his house. And they showed him sympathy and comforted him for all the evil that YHWH had brought upon him. And each one gave him a piece of silver (qesitah) and a ring of gold.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "qesitah" (H7192) is an ancient unit of monetary weight or value of uncertain exact amount, mentioned elsewhere only in Gen 33:19 and Josh 24:32 in connection with the patriarchs, reinforcing Job's patriarchal setting. The communal meal and gift-giving represent covenant restoration and social reintegration. The acknowledgment that YHWH "brought evil" (ra'ah, H7451) upon Job is an unguarded theological statement that does not shy away from YHWH's ultimate sovereignty over suffering.

verses.flow: And YHWH blessed the latter days of Job more than his beginning. And he had fourteen thousand sheep and six thousand camels and a thousand yoke of oxen and a thousand female donkeys.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he had seven sons and three daughters.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he called the name of the first Jemimah, and the name of the second Keziah, and the name of the third Keren-happuch.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The names of Job's daughters are uniquely given while the sons remain unnamed — a literary reversal of patriarchal convention. Jemimah (dove), Keziah (cassia/cinnamon — a precious spice), and Keren-happuch (horn of eye-paint/antimony) evoke beauty, fragrance, and ornamentation. These names suggest the restoration of flourishing and abundance after desolation. In the ANE, naming daughters in a legal or literary text often signaled elevated status within the household.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And in all the land there were found no women as beautiful as the daughters of Job. And their father gave them an inheritance among their brothers.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The granting of an inheritance (nachalah, H5159) to daughters alongside sons is extraordinary in the ancient legal context, paralleling the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27:1-11). This act of radical equity in the epilogue signals the re-ordering of creation under YHWH's blessing, a foretaste of the eschatological restoration where the marginalized receive their full portion. It quietly critiques the patriarchal assumptions that undergirded much of the friends' worldview.

verses.flow: And Job lived after this one hundred and forty years, and he saw his sons and his sons' sons, four generations.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And Job died, old and full of days.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Old and full of days" (zaqen uve'sa' yamim) is the identical phrase used of Abraham in Gen 25:8 and Isaac in Gen 35:29, marking Job's death as a patriarchal death — blessed, complete, and covenantally at rest. The book ends not with theodicy resolved theoretically but with a life restored experientially, anticipating the resurrection hope that suffering does not have the final word. Cf. (Jas 5:11); (Rom 8:18); (Rev 21:4).
